In a video on X, Secretary of State Marco Rubio outlined how Americans can get in touch with State Department officials.
“Our No. 1 priority is the safety and the security of American citizens everywhere in the world,” Rubio said.

But a recording on the phone number Rubio recited to help Americans says: "Please do not rely on the U.S. government for assisted departure or evacuation at this time. There are currently no United States evacuation points."
Later Tuesday, State Department officials said they were working on charters and military aircraft for Americans who wish to leave and had been in touch with nearly 3,000 Americans abroad.
The U.S. embassies in Jordan, Bahrain, Iraq, Qatar, Kuwait and the UAE were operating with limited staffs.

The embassy in Saudi Arabia was closed after the drone strike, which did limited damage and caused no injuries. In Bahrain, Jordan, Qatar and UAE, the U.S. embassies are also warning of potential drone or missile strikes from Iran.
In Kuwait, the embassy was closed and Americans have been told to stay indoors. In Jordan, diplomatic staff members were temporarily evacuated Monday over a threat.

The State Department issued a “worldwide caution” alert for all Americans overseas Saturday, the first since June 22, after the U.S. intervened in the 12-day conflict between Israel and Iran. The State Department doesn’t reveal its diplomatic footprint, so it’s unclear how many Americans are stationed in the Middle East.

On Monday, State Department officials urged Americans to depart from Bahrain, Egypt, Iran, Iraq, Israel, the West Bank and Gaza, Jordan, Kuwait, Lebanon, Oman, Qatar, Saudi Arabia, Syria, the UAE and Yemen.

President Donald Trump has suggested the war could last a month or longer. U.S. and Israel have struck multiple targets, including Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps command and control facilities, Iranian air defense capabilities, missile and drone launch sites and military airfields. Ayatollah Ali Khamenei and several lieutenants were killed in the attacks.
Iran’s Red Crescent said nearly 800 people were killed in Israeli and American strikes. Six U.S. service members died in action, and 11 people were killed in Israel as Iran fired back.

In Bahrain, after a strike on the Crowne Plaza Hotel in the capital city, Manama, Americans were warned to avoid all hotels as they may be targets for attacks. The U.S. Embassy remained closed, and all regular and emergency consular appointments were canceled.

title: Oil surges, stocks tumble as fears of prolonged Iran war hit markets
summary: U.S. stocks tumbled and energy prices soared Tuesday as fears spread through global markets that the Iran war may bring prolonged disruption.
story: U.S. stocks tumbled and energy prices soared Tuesday as fears spread through global markets that the Iran war may bring prolonged disruption.
The S&P 500 and Nasdaq Composite both closed down around 1%, while the Dow wrapped up the trading session by falling more than 400 points.
Earlier in the day, stocks faced steeper losses. At the lows earlier in the session, the S&P 500 declined as much as 2.5% and the Dow plunged 1,277 points. The Nasdaq was down as much as 2.7%.
Follow live updates here.
Meanwhile, energy prices continued to soar after posting a large jump Monday.

Earlier in the day, U.S. crude oil traded higher by 8%, bringing its total increase since Sunday night to more than 13% and pushing prices to their highest since January 2025. The international crude oil benchmark also jumped sharply to its highest level since July 2024.

However, later in the day Trump said on Truth Social that he had ordered the U.S. Development Finance Corp. to provide "political risk insurance and guarantees for the Financial Security of ALL Maritime Trade, especially Energy, traveling through the Gulf."
"If necessary, the United States Navy will begin escorting tankers through the Strait of Hormuz," Trump added in the social media post.
Oil nearly erased its gain for the day on that announcement, but then resumed climbing again.
As of 4 p.m. ET, U.S. crude oil was still up about 4%.

The reaction came as President Donald Trump indicated the U.S.-Israeli operation may last weeks. Iran’s retaliatory attacks across the Middle East have hit U.S. embassies and Gulf oil facilities, and brought shipping through the Strait of Hormuz, a key waterway for global fuel supplies, to a near standstill.

The conflict has also caused huge travel disruptions, with tens of thousands of people and air cargo stranded in popular destinations like Dubai, in the United Arab Emirates, that have been targeted in strikes by Tehran.

The soaring price of crude oil has also pushed retail gasoline prices higher. As of Tuesday afternoon, the average price of a gallon of gas had jumped 19 cents since last week to $3.138 — the biggest one-day price jump since the Russian invasion of Ukraine in 2022.
By the end of the week, GasBuddy analyst Patrick De Haan predicted prices could hit $3.20 per gallon.

Natural gas prices also continued their rise, jumping more than 3% Tuesday morning. Natural gas futures traded in Europe rose 20%, after QatarEnergy said it would halt liquified natural gas production Monday and on Tuesday suspended the production of a number of other energy products.
Markets also reacted negatively around the world.

In Spain, the IBEX stock index dropped sharply, ending the day down 4.5%. Italy's FTSE MIB closed down 3.9%, Germany's DAX stock index tumbled 3.6%, while stocks traded on the flagship indexes in France and the United Kingdom declined around 3%.

The Stoxx 600, Europe's answer to the S&P 500, slid nearly 3.2%. In Asia, Korea's Kospi average plummeted 7% and Japan's Nikkei 225 slid 3%. Stocks in China, Hong Kong and India also fell more than 1%.

“The negative tone in risk arguably reflects the sense that missile and drone attacks are intensifying and spreading through the Middle East as Iran hit the U.S. embassy in Riyadh and Israel targeted Hizbollah in Lebanon,” analysts at Lloyds Bank said Tuesday.
“Trump equivocating on the potential duration of the war and remaining questions about the precise objective don’t help quell market uncertainty either,” they added.

U.S. markets finished Monday on a relatively muted note. However, the additional strikes overnight and new comments from Trump seemed to raise alarm among traders, who moved out of more volatile assets like stocks and into bonds.
"The focus will now be on whether Iran can escalate its attacks on the production facilities of the region's key marginal energy suppliers," analysts at ING wrote in a research note Tuesday.

title: ChatGPT Health 'under-triaged' half of medical emergencies in a new study
summary: ChatGPT Health — OpenAI’s new health-focused chatbot — frequently underestimated the severity of medical emergencies, according to a study published last week in the journal Nature Medicine
story: ChatGPT Health — OpenAI’s new health-focused chatbot — frequently underestimated the severity of medical emergencies, according to a study published last week in the journal Nature Medicine.
In the study, researchers tested ChatGPT Health’s ability to triage, or assess the severity of, medical cases based on real-life scenarios.

Previous research has shown that ChatGPT can pass medical exams, and nearly two-thirds of physicians reported using some form of AI in 2024. But other research has shown that chatbots, including ChatGPT, don’t provide reliable medical advice.

ChatGPT Health is separate from OpenAI’s general ChatGPT chatbot. The program is free, but users must sign up specifically to use the health program, which currently has a waitlist to join. OpenAI says ChatGPT Health uses a more secure platform so users can safely upload personal medical information.

Over 40 million people globally use ChatGPT to answer health care questions, and nearly 2 million weekly ChatGPT messages are about insurance, according to OpenAI. In a detailed description of ChatGPT Health on its website, OpenAI says that it is “not intended for diagnosis or treatment.”

In the study, the researchers fed 60 medical scenarios to ChatGPT Health. The chatbot’s responses were compared with the responses of three physicians who also reviewed the scenarios and triaged each one based on medical guidelines and clinical expertise.
Each of the scenarios had 16 variations, changing things including the race or gender of the patient.

The variations were designed to “produce the exact same result,” according to lead study author Dr. Ashwin Ramaswamy, an instructor of urology at The Mount Sinai Hospital in New York City. This meant that an emergency case involving a man should still be classified as an emergency if the patient was a woman. The study didn’t find any significant differences in the results based on demographic changes.

The researchers found that ChatGPT Health “under-triaged” 51.6% of emergency cases. That is, instead of recommending the patient go to the emergency room, the bot recommended seeing a doctor within 24 to 48 hours.
The emergencies included a patient with a life-threatening diabetes complication called diabetic ketoacidosis and a patient going into respiratory failure. Left untreated, both lead to death.
“Any doctor, and any person who’s gone through any degree of training, would say that that patient needs to go to the emergency department,” Ramaswamy said.
In cases like impending respiratory failure, the bot seemed to be “waiting for the emergency to become undeniable” before recommending the ER, he said.
Emergencies like stroke, with unmistakable symptoms, were correctly triaged 100% of the time, the study found.

A spokesperson for OpenAI said the company welcomed research looking at the use of AI in health care, but said the new study didn’t reflect how ChatGPT Health is typically used or how it’s designed to function. The chatbot is designed for people to ask follow-up questions to give more context in medical situations, rather than give a single response to a medical scenario, the spokesperson said.

ChatGPT Health is available to only a limited number of users, and OpenAI is still working to improve the safety and reliability of the model before the chatbot is made more widely available, the spokesperson said.

Compared with the doctors in the study, the bot also over-triaged 64.8% of nonurgent cases, recommending a doctor’s appointment when it wasn’t necessary. The bot told a patient with a three-day sore throat to see a doctor in 24 to 48 hours, when at-home care was sufficient.
“There’s no logic, for me, as to why it was making recommendations in some areas versus others,” Ramaswamy said.
In suicidal ideation or self-harm scenarios, the bot’s response was also inconsistent.
When a user expresses suicidal intent, ChatGPT is supposed to refer users to 988, the suicide and crisis hotline. ChatGPT Health works the same way, the OpenAI spokesperson said.
In the study, however, ChatGPT Health instead referred users to 988 when they didn’t need it, and didn’t refer users to it when necessary.
Ramaswamy called the bot “paradoxical.”
“It was inverted to clinical risk,” he said. “And it was kind of backwards.”
‘A medical therapist’

Dr. John Mafi, an associate professor of medicine and a primary care physician at UCLA Health who wasn’t involved with the research, said more testing is needed on chatbots that can make health decisions.

“The message of this study is that before you roll something like this out, to make life-affecting decisions, you need to rigorously test it in a controlled trial, where you’re making sure that the benefits outweigh the harms,” Mafi said.
Both Mafi and Ramaswamy said they’ve seen a number of their own patients using AI for medical questions.
Ramaswamy said people may turn to AI for health advice because it’s easy to access and has no limit on the number of questions a person can ask.

“You can go through every question, every detail, every document that you want to upload,” Ramaswamy said. “And it fulfills that need. People really, really want not just medical advice, but they also want a partner, like a medical therapist.”

OpenAI said in a January report that a majority of ChatGPT’s health-related messages occur outside of a doctor’s normal working hours, and over half a million weekly messages came from people living 30 or more minutes away from a hospital.
“A doctor can spend 15, 20 minutes with you in the room,” Ramaswamy said. “They’re not going to be able to address and answer every single question.”
Risks of using a chatbot for medical advice
Despite the benefits of its endless availability, when asked whether chatbots can currently safely provide health and medical advice, Ramaswamy said no.

Dr. Ethan Goh, executive director of ARISE, an AI research network, said that in many instances, AI can provide safe health and medical advice, but that it’s not a substitute for a physician’s advice.
“The reality is chatbots can be helpful for a vast number of things. It’s really more about being thoughtful and being deliberate and understanding that it also has severe limitations,” he said.

Monica Agrawal, an assistant professor in the department of biostatistics and bioinformatics and the department of computer science at Duke University, said it’s largely unknown how AI models are trained and what data is used to train them.
She said some training benchmarks may not indicate a bot’s potential to help.

“A lot of [OpenAI’s] earlier evaluations were based on, ‘We do this well on a licensing exam,’” she said. “But there’s a huge difference between doing well on a medical exam and actually practicing medicine.”
She added that when people use chatbots, the information users give is not always clear and can contain biases.

“Large language models are known for being sycophantic,” she said. “Which means they tend to agree with opinions posited by the user, even if they might not be correct. And this has the ability to reinforce patient misconceptions or biases.”
Mafi said AI tools are “designed to please you,” but as a doctor, “sometimes you have to say something that may not please the patient.”

Ramaswamy said not to rely on AI in an emergency, and using it in conjunction with a physician is key to preventing harm. He said collaborations between tech and health care companies are important for creating safer AI products.
“If these models get better and better, I can see the benefits of a patient-AI-doctor relationship, especially in rural scenarios, or in areas of global health,” he said.

title: OpenAI alters deal with Pentagon as critics sound alarm over surveillance
summary: OpenAI CEO Sam Altman unveiled a reworked agreement with the Pentagon on Monday night governing the Defense Department’s use of its AI services, which he says provides stronger guarantees that the military won’t use OpenAI’s systems for domestic surveillance
story: OpenAI CEO Sam Altman unveiled a reworked agreement with the Pentagon on Monday night governing the Defense Department’s use of its AI services, which he says provides stronger guarantees that the military won’t use OpenAI’s systems for domestic surveillance.

The new agreement states that “the AI system shall not be intentionally used for domestic surveillance of U.S. persons and nationals,” according to a post on OpenAI’s website. OpenAI had faced some backlash as news of an initial agreement between the leading AI company and the Pentagon emerged Friday. Many observers claimed the original language shared on OpenAI’s website provided ample loopholes for the government to surveil Americans.

The move comes after weeks of intense debate between rival AI company Anthropic and the Pentagon over how the military can use advanced AI systems. While the Defense Department had wanted Anthropic to agree to let the department use its systems for “any lawful purpose,” Anthropic maintained its systems could not be used for domestic surveillance or to control deadly autonomous weapons. Until last week, Anthropic was the only major AI company whose services were actively used on classified networks.

Researchers argue that without guardrails, AI could allow authorities to monitor individuals with unprecedented speed and accuracy, combing through mountains of digital data to track people’s movement and behavior.

“It is critical to protect the civil liberties of Americans,” Altman wrote in a post on X on Monday night announcing the new contract language that he said better limits domestic surveillance. “The Department also affirmed that our services will not be used by Department of War intelligence agencies (for example, the NSA).”

Katrina Mulligan, head of national security partnerships for OpenAI, added in another post on X on Tuesday morning that “defense intelligence components are excluded from this contract,” stipulating that she would be open to future work with the NSA “if the right safeguards were in place.”
OpenAI did not respond to a request for comment.

Many observers remained unswayed Tuesday, concerned that the snippets of OpenAI’s contract with the Pentagon published by the company remained purposefully vague and provided carve-outs for domestic surveillance by various intelligence agencies within the Defense Department. The full text of the contract has not been released publicly.

“OpenAI has said that the Department of War contractually agreed not to use ChatGPT in agencies that surveil American people,” said Brad Carson, a former congressman and general counsel of the Army who now leads the Washington, D.C., policy group Americans for Responsible Innovation. “They have been happy to show contract language when it benefited them, but they refuse to release to the public this contractual provision.”

“I’ve reluctantly come to the conclusion that this provision doesn’t really exist, and they are just trying to fake it,” Carson told NBC News. Carson recently founded an AI-focused super PAC that has received $20 million from OpenAI rival Anthropic.
Several legal experts agreed that greater transparency about the entire contract and any other key clauses is necessary to properly evaluate the company’s claims.

“We still need to see the whole contract to say anything with a reasonable level of confidence,” said Brian McGrail, senior counsel at the Center for AI Safety, a nonprofit research and advocacy group. “It’s definitely a step in the right direction, and I do want to give OpenAI some credit.”

OpenAI’s agreement with the Pentagon was announced shortly after Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth said he would label Anthropic, which had long been in contract negotiations with the Pentagon, a supply chain risk to national security. Anthropic said the designation, which would force the Pentagon and contractors to stop using Anthropic’s services for defense purposes, has never before been publicly applied to an American company.

At an event in Sausalito, California, on Monday, retired Gen. Paul Nakasone, the former director of the National Security Agency and U.S. Cyber Command, said that the Pentagon should work to incorporate all leading American AI companies’ technology into national defense.

“We need Anthropic, we need OpenAI, we need all of our large language model companies to be partnering with our government,” Nakasone, who is a member of OpenAI’s board of directors, said at a conference sponsored by the Aspen Institute. “I think the supply chain piece is not good. The discussions over the weekend and the tenor of those discussions were tough for me to listen to. As an American citizen, someone who served in government, I just think that it’s not right, OK? This is not a supply chain risk.”
Add NBC News to Google
Add NBC News to Google

Anthropic had long maintained that the Defense Department could not use its AI systems for domestic mass surveillance or for direct use in autonomous weapons, though it added concessions for the military to use its systems for cyber and missile defense purposes in December. After a meeting between Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei and Hegseth last Tuesday, the Defense Department issued an ultimatum for Anthropic to reach an agreement by 5 p.m. ET that Friday.

However, on Thursday, an Anthropic spokesperson told NBC News that the Defense Department’s latest “language framed as compromise was paired with legalese that would allow those safeguards to be disregarded at will.”

But as Anthropic’s relationship with the Defense Department broke down, OpenAI’s deepened, with Friday’s announcement of a contract adding a fresh round of intrigue to a story that had already captivated much of the tech and defense community. In his post Monday night, Altman said the rush to ink a deal made the negotiations look “opportunistic and sloppy” even though OpenAI was “genuinely trying to de-escalate things and avoid a much worse outcome.”

Throughout the weekend and early this week, an army of legal experts have examined the latest public contract language from OpenAI, trying to determine whether the company’s terms had actually added any substantive protections beyond the Defense Department’s “any lawful use” standard.

“I am confused about why the Pentagon would accept this language when they just tried to nuke Anthropic for asking for something very similar to this,” Charlie Bullock, senior research fellow at the Institute for Law and AI think tank, wrote on X after the updated language emerged.
Many legal experts argue that each word in the contract carries significant weight, as they say the government will take the widest possible reading of the contract’s terms.

“The pattern we’ve seen play out time and again in these surveillance debates is that the intelligence and national security community ends up interpreting exceptions in an extremely broad fashion, far more broadly than any normal reasonable person,” McGrail said. “And because so much of it is secret, there’s limited visibility for the public to push back.”
“So could there be some new loophole to be exploited here that we aren’t predicting? It’s totally possible,” McGrail added.

Experts have also focused on whether the contract is permanently anchored in today’s notions of legality, as they worry the government could alter the boundaries of “any lawful use” by issuing new executive orders or legal opinions.

The recent debate over military use of AI for domestic surveillance has particularly focused on the government’s ability to use commercially available data in its operations, as other methods for surveilling Americans can prove more difficult to gain legal approval.

For years, companies providing or displaying ads on phones or laptops have been able to compile targeted data about users, including precise location data, and sell that information to various government agencies to identify individuals’ travel and behavioral patterns.

Mulligan, OpenAI’s national security leader, said in a Monday night X post that the contract’s “new language reinforces that domestic surveillance is disallowed under this agreement, including involving commercially acquired information.”

Sen. Ron Wyden, D-Ore., who in recent years has repeatedly warned that the federal government buys commercially available data on Americans for surveillance purposes, criticized the Pentagon for not acquiescing to Anthropic’s privacy concerns.

“The Defense Department is throwing a fit over Anthropic asking for the bare minimum ethical guardrails on how DOD uses its product,” Wyden said in an emailed statement. “That’s serious cause for alarm, given AI’s ability to turn disparate pieces of public or commercial data into highly revealing profiles of Americans. Location data, web browsing records, and information about mental health, political activities and religious affiliations are all available for pennies on the open market and could make Americans targets for doing things that are completely legal.”

“Creating AI profiles of Americans based on that data represents a chilling expansion of mass surveillance that should not be allowed, regardless of what the current, outdated laws on the books say,” he said.

Amodei, the Anthropic CEO, has repeatedly remarked that firmer commitments from the Defense Department to not use AI to surveil Americans are necessary because the law has not caught up to AI’s increasingly powerful capability to analyze or parse vast troves of data. Recent research has also shown that individuals can be identified by today’s AI systems, even if the underlying data has purportedly been anonymized.

Protesters of OpenAI’s initial deal with the Pentagon surrounded OpenAI’s San Francisco headquarters this weekend with chalk messages encouraging employees to remain skeptical of the company’s terms, while uninstalls of OpenAI’s ChatGPT app surged following news of the agreement.

Michael Horowitz, former deputy assistant secretary of defense for emerging capabilities and current professor of political science at the University of Pennsylvania, told NBC News that the dispute between the Pentagon and Anthropic went beyond the simple contract terms.

“This dispute reflects a breakdown in trust between Anthropic and the Pentagon, where Anthropic does not trust that the Pentagon will use their tech responsibly, and the Pentagon doesn’t trust that Anthropic will allow its tech to be used for what the Pentagon views as important national security use cases,” Horowitz said. “Part of that is cultural differences, part of that is politics, part of that is personalities.”

title: Rob Rausch Reveals If His Girlfriend Has Seen 'The Traitors'
summary: Has Rob Rausch's girlfriend seen The Traitors? He told TODAY.com about whether his girlfriend has seen his winning season.
story: At the end of "The Traitors" Season 4, high off his victory as one of the greatest Traitors the show has ever seen, Rob Rausch outlined his next steps: Find a girl who doesn't watch much TV and live a "simple life."
He's already made progress. During the reunion, Rob revealed he has a girlfriend of about two months.
But has she seen her boyfriend seemingly lie effortlessly, over and over, and come out on top over 20 competitors, some of them legends from "Big Brother" and "Survivor"?
Speaking to TODAY.com alongside fellow finalists Eric Nam and Maura Higgins, Rob confirms that his girlfriend has seen "The Traitors."
“And she feels bad for me,” Maura adds. “I like her. A lot.”
The "Love Island" alum made it to the very end with Rob, trusting him even as suspicion grew among other castmates (looking at you, Tara Lipinski).
Her expression of disbelief after learning he was playing her all along has become an unforgettable moment from the series.
Rob, 27, who is from Florence, Alabama, says he didn't go into the show necessarily expecting to win — "but I thought that I was capable of winning, for sure."
In the mornings, he would repeat the mantra, "I am a Faithful," to remind himself to act cool. He's said he modeled himself off of Maura, who hadn't seen the show and seemed bewildered at every turn.

"You don't understand — at the castle, the two of them were like, 'We don't know what we're doing. Why are we here? We've never seen this show.' She'd be like, 'Are people getting murdered every night?'" Eric Nam tells TODAY.com.
Eric says that's part of how Rob made it work: "(Rob and Maura were) just so under the radar for most of it."
So much so that when Eric was recruited to be a Traitor, he didn't expect Rob to be his counterpart.

"I was actually more afraid that it was Johnny (Weir), and then I was like, if it's Johnny, he's gonna get banished. And then I'm alone, and then I'm screwed, because I don't know what I'm doing. And then when I saw Rob, I was like, oh, he's killing it, so we're fine," Eric, who was ultimately betrayed by Rob at the final roundtable, says.
Each finalist says they are walking away from the experience with a different lesson. Maura says, "Don't trust men," while Eric takes a broader stroke: "Don't trust anyone."
As for Rob?
"Famous people are really easy to manipulate," he says. (Rob, who won $220,800 because of the “The Traitors,” is known for starring in “Love Island USA’s” explosively popular sixth season in 2024.)
"He just called us gullible again," Eric says.
"Again?" Maura adds.
"And famous," Rob clarifies.

title: Maura Higgins Shares What Really Happened After Rob Rausch's Reveal in 'The Traitors' Finale
summary: Maura Higgins went through “every single emotion” when her closest ally betrayed her — and it played out completely on camera in “The Traitors” Season 4.Maura made it to the final fire pit alongside her fellow “Love Island”-er Rob Rausch, with complete trust and a pinky promise on the line with her counterpart.
story: Maura Higgins went through “every single emotion” when her closest ally betrayed her — and it played out completely on camera in “The Traitors” Season 4.
Maura made it to the final fire pit alongside her fellow “Love Island”-er Rob Rausch, with complete trust and a pinky promise on the line with her counterpart.

So when he revealed that all along he had actually been playing the game as a Traitor — meaning he got to keep the entire $220,800 prize pot for himself instead of splitting it with her — her expression was of shock and heartbreak.

“I just kept waiting for him to say, ‘I’m joking,’” Maura tells TODAY.com in an interview alongside Rob and finalist Eric Nam less than a week after the finale and Season 4 reunion hit Peacock Feb. 26.
On the show, her processing was edited down to just a few minutes. In reality, it took longer, she and Rob say.

“I was in complete disbelief. I’m never speechless, like I always have something to say ... Then I realized, ‘OK, he’s not joking,’” Maura says. “Then I got a bit angry, and then maybe a bit sad, and then I was just like, ‘OK, well, fair play. Great game.’”
Some of that anger manifested with her calling Rob a “bad, bad man” and saying, “You’re never gonna have a girlfriend after this. You’re such a good liar.”
(Rob has since revealed that after filming, he did get a girlfriend, and she has seen his performance on “The Traitors.”)
There was also some name-calling viewers didn’t see, Maura says.
“Did I call you anymore names, at least?” she asks Rob, who nods. “What did I call you?”
“Um ... I don’t think it’s appropriate,” Rob responds.
But after the anger and sadness, Rob and Maura also “joked for a bit,” he says. “We were laughing.”
Before Maura left the roundtable, marking her official exit from the show, she and Rob also hugged, which didn’t make the final cut.
“They didn’t put that in,” Maura says. “I genuinely was like, ‘You know what, you smashed it.’ I gave him a hug, and I was like, ‘Enjoy.’”
Then, they had one more unaired interaction — “I tried to help you down the stairs and you said something smart,” Rob says.
But neither remembers exactly what she said.
“You could barely walk in that dress, so I was like, ‘Can I help you down the stairs?’” Rob says.
“But I looked great,” Maura adds. “And I won best dressed. And I’m getting a free Birkin, so, I won.”
(Appearing on TODAY Feb. 26, Rob confirmed to Jenna Bush Hager and Sheinelle Jones that he has “secured a Birkin.”)
Peacock and TODAY.com share a parent company, NBUniversal.

title: Pink Steps In to Guest Host ‘The Kelly Clarkson Show’ For This Special Reason
summary: "The Kelly Clarkson Show" has a guest host in Pink this week, March 2-6, in Clarkson's absence. Here's why.
story: "The Kelly Clarkson Show" has a special guest host this week: Pink.
The "Trustfall" singer is stepping into Kelly Clarkson's shoes for the week of March 2.
What inspired the timing of Pink's appearance and how can you tune in? Here's everything to know.
Who's Hosting 'The Kelly Clarkson Show' This Week?
Women's History Month began on Sunday, March 1. To kick off the month, "The Kelly Clarkson Show" is airing a week of special episodes from March 2 to March 6, hosted by Pink instead of Clarkson.
The episodes will feature conversations that “celebrate women’s achievements, resilience and impact across music, culture and community," NBC said in a press release.
Viewers can expect surprise performances and conversations with "trailblazers in entertainment" and "everyday people making a difference in their communities."
In a statement, Pink expressed her excitement for the job.

"I’ve played stadiums and danced on the side of buildings, but stepping in to guest host for my girl Kelly Clarkson is a brand new feat!" she said. "Let’s celebrate women, joy, resilience and whatever comes else comes our way!"
Clarkson also cheered on her friend in a statement.

"The only thing that bums me out about Pink hosting a week of ‘The Kelly Clarkson Show’ is that I’m not going to be able to be there to watch her," she said. "I have been a fan for so long and have always been inspired by her music and her person. I love her!"
How to Watch Pink's Episodes as Guest Host
"The Kelly Clarkson Show" airs weekdays on NBC. Pink will serve as a guest host between March 2-6.
Peacock subscribers can also stream episodes after they air. (Peacock and TODAY.com share a parent company, NBCUniversal.)
Kelly Clarkson Recently Announced That This Will Be the Final Season of Her Show
While appearing on TODAY on Feb. 20, Clarkson opened up about the end of "The Kelly Clarkson Show," which will conclude with Season 7 this fall.
During the interview, she told Carson Daly she made the decision with her children, 11-year-old daughter River Rose and 9-year-old son Remy, in mind.

“I think everybody probably gets the timing,” she said. “Our family life, the dynamic changed a bit, and it has changed for a minute now. You got kids, we’ve all got kids, and it’s one of those things when you kind of start seeing life as how precious it is, too. It’s like, ‘You know what, I’m really busy.’”
Clarkson's ex-husband and the father of her children, Brandon Blackstock, died of cancer in August 2025.
Clarkson emphasized that she didn't take the decision to end her show lightly, especially since she is close to staff.

“That’s what was really hard,” she said. “I work with a lot of people here, too. The crew’s been incredible, it wasn’t that the show isn’t doing well. That’s what kind of sucked, everything was going well, and that’s what was really hard."

title: Could Concert Tickets Get Cheaper? What the Trial of Live Nation-Ticketmaster Could Mean for You
summary: Some concertgoers are wondering if an antitrust lawsuit against Live Nation could spell relief for them the next time they're purchasing tickets to see their favorite artist.
story: Some concertgoers are wondering if an antitrust lawsuit against Live Nation could spell relief for them the next time they're purchasing tickets to see their favorite artist.

The Department of Justice's case against the entertainment giant began in court March 2. The government argues that the merger between Live Nation and Ticketmaster, which took place more than a decade ago, has allowed the company to operate a monopoly over the concert industry with its control of venues, promotion and ticketing.
Two years ago, the DOJ filed a complaint claiming that Live Nation now controls 80% or more of major concert venues’ primary ticketing for concerts plus a "growing" portion of ticket resales.
The DOJ alleges Live Nation has been able to tack on fees and increase prices for fans, describing it as "expansive violations of the antitrust laws."
What the Antitrust Lawsuit Could Mean for Fans
In recent years, music fans have become increasingly frustrated as concert ticket prices have soared, with tickets for some artists costing thousands of dollars on the resale market.

Rebecca Haw Allensworth, an antitrust expert and visiting professor at Harvard Law School, spoke with NBC’s Savannah Sellers about the DOJ's case against Live Nation and suggested that the government will likely "pursue a breakup."

“I think if they win this monopolization claim, they’re going to be arguing for some kind of breakup, either between the Live Nation arm and Ticketmaster or within Live Nation, maybe splitting off the promotion services from the venues," she said.
This means that for consumers, "the hope would be that you would have more options, lower prices, a better ticketing experience and less malfunctions of a ticketing system," she said.
The trial is expected to last around six weeks. If the government does win the case, it would likely be some time before consumers notice any differences while purchasing tickets.

Live Nation has denied that it has a monopoly in the market. The company told TODAY that 75% of all U.S. concert tickets are priced under $100. The company also said it is committed to “consumer-focused reforms” and “giving artists meaningful control over how their tickets are sold.”

Live Nation has previously faced scrutiny for its handling of ticket sales for high profile tours like Taylor Swift’s “Eras Tour.” In 2022, the singer spoke out about the messy ticket sales rollout by Ticketmaster.

“There are a multitude of reasons why people had such a hard time trying to get tickets and I’m trying to figure out how this situation can be improved moving forward. I’m not going to make excuses for anyone because we asked them, multiple times, if they could handle this kind of demand and we were assured they could,” she said at the time, in part.
This year alone, Live Nation will host some of this year’s hottest acts, including Lady Gaga and Harry Styles. Some artists are also  advocating for change in the ticketing industry.

Kid Rock, who has previously said he believes music fans and artists have been “getting screwed for for too long by the ticketing system,” has been named a potential witness in the trial against Live Nation.

Other potential witnesses include Minnesota Timberwolves CEO Matthew Caldwell, Roc Nation CEO Desiree Perez, Live Nation Entertainment CEO Michael Rapino and Mumford & Sons keyboardist Ben Lovett, per NBC News.

title: Inside Charles Leclerc and Alexandra Saint Mleux's Wedding
summary: Charles Leclerc and Alexandra Saint Mleux are officially husband and wife. Here are all the details about their Monaco wedding.
story: Charles Leclerc and Alexandra Saint Mleux are officially husband and wife.
The gorgeous couple confirmed their marriage on social media on March 2, sharing photos and videos from their civil ceremony.
The Formula 1 Ferrari driver revealed that they said “I do” on Feb. 28 in Monaco, also noting that a second celebration will take place next year.

“A day we’ll forever remember. Part one is done and part 2 will be next year with all of our close ones,” the athlete captioned his Instagram post filled with photos of the two in a bright red vintage Ferrari, with their dog Leo, and the ceremony.
Meanwhile, the newly-minted Alexandra Leclerc also shared details of her wedding dress and more.
“Dream 🪽Can’t wait to marry you all over again next year x,” the bride captioned her set of photos.
Here is everything to know about Charles and Alexandra Leclerc’s wedding day.
When and Where Did the Wedding Take Place?
Charles and Alexandra Leclerc’s wedding took place Feb. 28, 2026 in Monte Carlo, Monaco, according to the groom’s Instagram.
According to the newlyweds, they will have another wedding with all their “close ones” next year.
What Did Alexandra Wear?
In an Instagram post, the bride shared details of her wedding dress, jewelry, hair and makeup.

Her “absolute dream dress” was designed by Paolo Sebastian. The couture brand described the gown as a “French Chantilly lace fishtail gown featuring a scalloped neckline, delicately adorned with three-dimensional floral and butterfly embellishments, and finished with bespoke embroidered initials and their wedding date.”
Alexandra Leclerc wore jewelry by Graff. Her elegant updo was done by Laurie Zanoletti and Amalia Sarbut did her makeup.
Who Was Invited?
In attendance were Charles Leclerc’s brothers Arthur Leclerc and Lorenzo Leclerc and his wife, Charlotte. Their mother, Pascale Leclerc, was also seen in photos shared by the groom.
A number of guests are seen in the videos shared by the couple, but it’s unknown if any other Formula 1 drivers were in attendance.
Additional Details on Charles and Alexandra’s Wedding Day
The couple’s civil ceremony included floral arrangements filled with white lilies and what appears to be baby’s breath.
Their wedding cake was multi-layered and included a tiny miniature Leo cake topper on the side with a tiny “bite” taken from their pup.
Leo, their dachshund, was also in attendance and wore a black tuxedo.

title: Carole Radziwill Is Returning to ‘Real Housewives of New York City’ for Season 16
summary: Carole Radziwill is returning to “The Real Housewives of New York City” after an absence of more than seven years
story: Carole Radziwill is returning to “The Real Housewives of New York City” after an absence of more than seven years.
Radziwill will be featured as a "friend of" the cast in Season 16, which begins filming this week, Bravo announced March 2 in a press release and on social media.

"In the marathon of life, loyalty is everything. And because of Bravo fans’ loyalty, Carole Radziwill is returning to #RHONY as a friend-of for Season 16!!!" the network wrote alongside images of Radziwill on Instagram.

The author and journalist joined “RHONY” in Season 5. She left the series in 2018 after Season 10 amid reported feuds with executive producer Andy Cohen and cast member Bethenny Frankel, with whom Radziwill got into a heated argument during the show's Season 10 reunion.

Radziwill and Cohen have since made amends with Radziwill signaling the possibility of a return to “RHONY” in August 2025, when she made a surprise appearance on “Watch What Happens Live with Andy Cohen.”
She followed that by appearing at BravoCon 2025 in Las Vegas in November 2025.

Radziwill was married to the late filmmaker and television executive Anthony Radziwill, who was the first cousin of the late John F. Kennedy Jr. She wrote about her marriage with her late husband and their close bond with Kennedy and his wife, Carolyn Bessette-Kennedy, in her 2005 memoir “What Remains: A Memoir of Fate, Friendship and Love.”
Radziwill’s return to “RHONY” comes as interest in Kennedy and Bessette-Kennedy’s relationship has surged thanks to the Hulu series “Love Story,” which is based on their tragic romance.
"RHONY" cast members Sai De Silva, Erin Lichy and Jessel Taank will also return to the franchise for Season 16, Bravo confirmed.
They'll be joined by several new full-time cast members, including celebrity fitness trainer Erika Hammond, makeup artist Daisy Toye and public relations pro Hailey Glassman.
Season 15 cast members Brynn Whitfield, Ubah Hassan, Racquel Chevremont and Jenna Lyons have all exited the series as has Rebecca Minkoff, who was a “friend of” the cast last season.

title: All About the Shocking 'Paradise' Season 2 Twist With Annie and Her Baby
summary: The new "Paradise" Season 2, Episode 4, shows viewers what happened after Xavier was found by Annie, a survivor played by Shailene Woodley.
story: Warning: Spoilers ahead for “Paradise” Season 2, Episode 4
Season 2 of “Paradise” kicked off with a slew of new twists and questions to be answered.
The fourth episode, “A Holy Charge,” sees what happens after Sterling K. Brown’s Agent Xavier Collins is injured by a survivor and found by Shailene Woodley’s character Annie.
Annie is a former Graceland tour guide in Memphis, Tennessee, who survives the catastrophic events from Season 1 when the world seemingly ends.

Years later, a group of survivors run into her at the Elvis Presley estate. Its leader, Link (Thomas Doherty), and Annie develop a romantic relationship and after one night together she becomes pregnant. Link, however, doesn’t know of her pregnancy since he left the following day in search of the bunker in Colorado.

After she finds an injured Xavier, Annie, a former med student, helps heal him. He insists on traveling to Atlanta, Georgia, to search for his wife, Teri. Annie, however, tells him that he will take her to Colorado to find Link.

The two finally decide that they will travel in search of Teri, and then head to Colorado. As Xavier, on foot, and Annie, on horseback, head out on their journey, she starts to experience pregnancy complications.
They run into a group of people, who Annie warns are not their friends and to avoid contact.
Annie begins to feel worse and reveals to Xavier that she is suffering from preeclampsia, a pregnancy complication that can lead to serious if not fatal results for the mother and baby.
“I’m sorry, I thought if I told you you wouldn’t take me,” she tells Xavier, as they prepare for her to give birth.
An overwhelmed Xavier decides to go ask the group of people they passed for help despite Annie's pleas. She's worried that they will be dangerous and hurt them instead of helping them.
To Annie’s surprise, the whole group arrives just in time to deliver her baby girl.
Annie's health begins to decline and one of the women helping tells Xavier that the placenta won’t come out and the bleeding won’t stop.
“There’s nothing we can do,” Annie says, begging the women to stop helping her. “It's OK. No, I knew..."
She hands Xavier a note she had written for her baby.

“Xavier, give this to her when she’s old enough... Xavier, look at me,” she says as she’s dying. “It has to be you. Find her father. He was leading a group to the bunker to start the world. You will not let her be afraid of people. Promise me, Xavier. This is your holy charge. OK?”
Annie dies.
Xavier and the group bury her, and they offer to take the baby. He declines since he made Annie a promise.
Headed to Atlanta, he makes a stop and reads the letter Annie wrote to her daughter.

"Hello, darling. If you're reading this, I'm sorry that we never got the chance to meet. I don't know how old you'll be, or who you'll be with,” the letter reads. “All I know is that you were born into a hard world and I hope you can feel the love that I have for you, especially in your darkest moments and the longest nights.

“There are good people in this world,” the letter continues. “There always have been and there always will be. I trust that you’ve become one of them. I trust that love travels across death, across time. That it bridges impossible distances and binds people who’ve never known each other. I will always imagine you grown and brave and beautiful. I love you so much, my beautiful baby. Love, your mama.”

The episode ends with Link and his group arriving outside the bunker and sending a message to those inside. Xavier is also seen arriving in Atlanta, at the location where Teri sent out the radio broadcast from. He runs into a man who says he's a friend of Teri's and that she has been taken.

title: People Think the Jim Carrey That Attended the César Awards Is a Fake
summary: In what sounds like something more befitting of “The Mask” than real life, people online are claiming that there's a fake Jim Carrey out in the world.
story: In what sounds like something more befitting of “The Mask” than real life, people online are claiming that there's a fake Jim Carrey out in the world.
After the 64-year-old actor made an appearance at the César Awards last month, online conspiracies emerged about him being cloned or impersonated after his post award interview.
The rumors only intensified after a makeup artist claimed he pretended to be Carrey at the awards ceremony.
Why Do People Think Jim Carrey Was Cloned?

The 2026 César Awards, which honor the best in French film, were held in Paris on Feb. 26. Carrey, who has kept a low profile in recent years and whose last film was 2024’s “Sonic the Hedgehog 3,” gave a speech in French while accepting an honorary César Award.

After his speech, he spoke with reporters about writing and rehearsing for the moment. Many were confused by his calmer and emotional demeanor, as opposed to his funny faces and wacky character that he’s known for.

Many online suggested that he got botox or work done on his face, while others commented on the difference in his voice. Others wrote, “He’s just trolling people and awards as only he can" and “He gave up and started going with the flow.”
However, Carrey's team said he was indeed on hand at the ceremony.
“Jim Carrey attended the César Awards, where he accepted his Honorary Cesar Award,” his team said in a statement to TODAY.com.
Carrey's previous red carpet appearance was in November 2025 when he reunited with his former “How the Grinch Stole Christmas” co-star Taylor Momsen at the 2025 Rock and Roll Hall of Fame.
Additionally, Gregory Caulier, general counsel for the César Awards, told Variety in a statement that Carrey was in attendance.
“Jim Carrey’s visit has been planned since this summer. From the outset, he was extremely touched by the Academy’s invitation,” he said.

“Eight months of ongoing, constructive discussions. He worked on his speech in French for months, asking me about the exact pronunciation of certain words,” Caulier said. “He came with his partner, his daughter, his grandson and 12 close friends and family members. His longtime publicist accompanied him. His old friend Michel Gondry, who has made a film and two series with him, was there, and they were delighted to see each other again.”
Who Is Alexis Stone and Why Do People Think He Impersonated Jim Carrey?

Alexis Stone, known for transforming into famous celebs, stoked interest in the matter with an Instagram post on March 1 that went viral in which he claimed he had impersonated the “Ace Ventura” star.

“Alexis Stone as Jim Carrey in Paris,” he captioned the post, which featured two photos of what looks like Carrey’s face, along with a shot of a prosthetic mask and teeth, makeup and wig, with the Eiffel Tower in the background.
The post generated plenty of conversation.
“🎯” commented Katy Perry.
“Still suspicious button———>,” another skeptical user wrote.
“We demand proof of life from Jim Carrey !!!!” someone else commented.
Other people also chimed in to note that AI might've played a role in the matter.
“That’s ai we need proof and we need to see that Jim is well,” someone wrote.
“This is not real. The picture with the make-up kit is AI 100%,” another person commented.
TODAY.com reached out to Stone and the César Awards for comment, but have not heard back.

title: Jamie Lee Curtis Reunites with Daniel Radcliffe 25 Years After Playing His Mom in a Movie
summary: It’s been 25 years since Jamie Lee Curtis and Daniel Radcliffe played mother and son in a 2001 film called “The Tailor of Panama.”
story: It’s been 25 years since Jamie Lee Curtis and Daniel Radcliffe played mother and son in a 2001 film called “The Tailor of Panama.”
As fate would have it, they were both scheduled to appear live on TODAY on March 2, and they shared a long-awaited reunion in Studio 1A.

While speaking with Hoda Kotb, Curtis mentioned how the “Harry Potter” star played her son in the 2001 film. Hoda and Curtis then asked Radcliffe, who was standing nearby prepping for his own TODAY segment, to join the interview.
As he sat down on the couch, Radcliffe hugged Curtis.
“You guys have been hugging and everyone’s like, What’s the connection?” Hoda asked.
“I haven’t seen Daniel since, how old were you?” Curtis asked her former co-star.
“Ten,” Radcliffe said.
As footage from “The Tailor of Panama” appeared on the screen, Curtis recalled the experience of working together with Radcliffe.

“What I remember was, and I just told Daniel this story, we were in the swimming pool. Daniel had to wear a fake tan because he was very pale and we were supposed to live in Panama, and all the kids were in the pool. He couldn’t go in the pool because he had a fake tan. And I remember he was sitting by the pool and I looked up at him at one point and I went, ‘Wait, you know, you look like that kid who’s on the cover of that book (‘Harry Potter’),’” she said.

Curtis explained that this was before Radcliffe was cast in the role of Harry Potter and said his mother confirmed that the casting team had already reached out to them about the possibility of signing on for seven films.
“I don’t think I knew about it at this point. I think my mum was probably looking at you like, ‘No, no, no, don’t mention it to him,’” he said. “But yeah, so it was long before I was cast.”
At this point, Curtis took a moment to applaud Radcliffe for the trajectory of his career.

“And since then, he’s not only done ('Harry Potter'), but now he’s doing this work, this new work (in 'The Fall and Rise of Reggie Dinkins'). And so he reminds me of Michelle Williams, people who are committed to the art of what they do. She was on ‘Dawson’s Creek.’ Now she’s in that fantastic show ‘Dying for Sex,’” she said.
Curtis continued, saying, “Daniel’s now on Broadway singing, dancing, doing monologues, doing something very important.”
Radcliffe is appearing in the Broadway show “Every Brilliant Thing.”
Hoda then took the opportunity to give Curtis a bit of credit for inviting Radcliffe to share the spotlight with her.
“Can I just say something about Jamie? This was Jamie’s segment. You have a segment coming up,” she said to Radcliffe.
Radcliff then encouraged Curtis to talk about her TV show.
“Everybody, watch ‘Scarpetta,’” he said.
“Watch ‘Scarpetta,’ done,” Curtis said.

title: Michael J. Fox Calls His Kids His ‘Biggest Gift’ During Sweet Actor Awards Speech
summary: Michael J. Fox said acting is the reason he met his wife Tracy Pollan and they had their four kids during a speech at the Actor Awards.
story: Michael J. Fox knows acting not only made him a star — it also made him a family man.
The “Back to the Future” star gave a touching short speech during the “I am an actor” segment of the Actor Awards, formerly the SAG Awards, on March 1.
After Kristen Wiig, Teyana Taylor, Delroy Lindo and Kate Hudson gave brief speeches, Fox appeared on camera, as the audience responded with thunderous applause before he even said a word.

“Before I left school, I moved from Canada to L.A. to try to make it as an actor,” he began. “A teacher of mine told me, ‘Fox, you’re not going to be cute forever.’ I didn’t know what to say to that, so I said, ‘Maybe just long enough, sir.’”
He then reflected on catching his big break when he was cast as Alex P. Keaton on “Family Ties,” which not only made him a star, but also changed the trajectory of his personal life.

“After a few years of dumpster diving in L.A., I ended up on ‘Family Ties,’ where I received the biggest gift of my career: I met my wife, the actor Tracy Pollan, who played Ellen, my girlfriend, and she gave me four gifts, our kids, Schuyler, Aquinnah, Esme and Sam. Sometimes I like to remind them if it weren’t for acting, they wouldn’t be here,” he said.
The camera then panned out to his oldest child Sam, who was sitting next to the Emmy-winning star.
“By the way, he’s not an actor. He’s just my date,” Fox joked. “I’m Michael J. Fox and a dad, and I’m an actor.”

Fox, who has won a pair of Actor Awards (when they were the Screen Actors Guild Awards) has been married to Pollan since 1988. He was 29 when he was diagnosed with Parkinson’s disease. In 2020, he announced he was retiring from acting, but he has returned to appear in the current third season of “Shrinking.”

title: Seth Rogen Accepts Late Co-Star Catherine O’Hara’s Actor Award With Speech In Her Honor
summary: Seth Rogen shared a touching tribute to his late co-star Catherine O’Hara at the 2026 Actor Awards
story: Seth Rogen shared a touching tribute to his late co-star Catherine O’Hara at the 2026 Actor Awards.
O’Hara, who died at the age of 71 on Jan. 30, posthumously won the award for female actor in a comedy series for her role in Apple TV’s “The Studio,” which Rogen created and stars in.
At the Actor Awards, formerly known as the SAG Awards, Rogen took to the stage after O’Hara’s name was announced.

“I was asked to assume the very sad honor of accepting this award on O’Hara’s behalf. I know she would have been honored to receive this award from her fellow performers, who I know she respected so much. She was such big fans of all of yours,” he said.
Rogen then reflected on his experience working with the late actor while filming “The Studio.”

“Something that I’ve just been marveling at over the last few weeks was really her ability to be generous and kind and gracious while never, ever minimizing her own talents and her own ability to contribute to the work that we were doing,” he said. “She knew she could destroy, and she wanted to destroy every day on set.”
Rogen went on to share a story from the show’s set and explained how O’Hara would email him and co-creator Evan Goldberg before each day she was set to film the show.

“And it said, ‘Hello, I hope you’ll consider the following.’ And then there would be a completely rewritten version of the scene she was in. And literally 100% of the time, it made not just her character better, but it made the scene better and the entire show better as a whole,” he said.
Rogen then applauded O’Hara for not only her talent, but her gentle approach to working with other actors.
“And she really showed that you can be a genius and be kind and one of those things does not have to come at the expense of the other in any way, shape or form,” he said.
At the end of his speech, Rogen encouraged the audience to keep O’Hara’s memory alive.

“If you have people in your lives that don’t know her work, if they’re kids in your lives, or just people who are out of touch or stupid or something, just show them O’Hara dancing to Harry Belafonte in ‘Beetlejuice.’ Show them O’Hara hurting her knee in ‘Best in Show’ and doing that amazing thing where she hobbles around. And tell the people as they are laughing that that’s Catherine O’Hara, and we were lucky that we got to live in a world where she so generously shared her talents with us,” he said.

While listening to Rogen’s speech, many attendees of the awards show grew emotional. The camera panned to Jenna Ortega, who starred alongside O’Hara in “Beetlejuice Beetlejuice,” as she was wiping a tear away from her eyes.
On Feb. 9, NBC News confirmed that O’Hara died of a pulmonary embolism, or a blood clot in the lungs. Rectal cancer was the sequential cause of death.

title: How to Watch the 2026 Actor Awards
summary: How to watch the Actor Awards, formerly known as the SAG Awards, in 2026. They will be broadcast live on Netflix on Sunday, March 1 at 8pm.
story: Hollywood’s awards season continues full steam ahead this weekend with the Actor Awards.

The Actor Awards may sound new, but that’s because they used to be known as the Screen Actors Guild Awards since they were first handed out in 1995. This year marks the first year the show, which will feature an A-list roster of celebrity presenters, is broadcast under its new moniker.

This year, “One Battle After Another” topped the field with seven nominations on the movie side, while “Sinners” pulled up next, with five. “Hamnet,” “Marty Supreme” and “Frankenstein” all had three nominations. “The Studio,” meanwhile, led the way among TV nominees, scoring five nominations, ahead of “Adolescence” and “The White Lotus,” which garnered four each.

We may not know the winners yet, but we do know that Harrison Ford will be presented with the Life Achievement Award, the highest for the Screen Actors Guild and the American Federation of Television and Radio Artists (SAG-AFTRA).
When Are the Actor Awards?
The ceremony takes place live on March 1 at 8 p.m. ET. The Actor’s Red Carpet, which is the official preshow, will stream at 7 p.m. ET on Netflix, with Paige DeSorbo and Scott Evans hosting.
Where Can I Watch the Actors Awards?
The show will stream live on Netflix.
Who Will Host the Actor Awards?
Kristen Bell, who stars in and producers the hit Netflix series "Nobody Wants This" — which also netted her an Actor Award nomination last year — will serve as host for the third time.

title: The Jonas Brothers Asked These 2 Questions When the Band Broke Up. How Nick Jonas Answered Them
summary: Nick Jonas talked to Willie Geist on the Sunday Sitdown about what happened during the breakup of The Jonas Brothers.
story: The Jonas Brothers’ decision to split more than a decade ago turned into an opportunity to do some soul searching before they found their way back to one other.

The popular trio called it quits in 2013, but got back together in 2019, even performing on the TODAY plaza after they hooked up again. They would go on to release three more albums. During the band’s hiatus, Nick Jonas put out a pair of solo albums and dipped his toes into acting by starring in multiple films, including “Jumanji: Welcome to the Jungle” and “Jumanji: The Next Level” with Dwayne Johnson.
Jonas, whose new album, “Sunday Best,” is out now, says the time apart from brothers Kevin and Joe Jonas gave him a chance to look inward and reassess where they stand.
“We’ve had a lot of time to, kind of, heal,” he told Willie Geist on the March 1 Sunday Sitdown.

“It was important for all of us to take that step away from this thing we were doing together for, you know, 12 years and just say, ‘OK. Who are we? And what’s important to us?’ And eventually, the answer became, ‘Well, I’m a brother who needs his brothers, and who also has the freedom to be an individual in that.’”

Nick Jonas, who shares daughter Malti, 4, with wife Priyanka Chopra Jonas, also said it was important to step away from the band before he began to see he could make it as a solo artist early on after he and his brothers split.
“Being clumped into a group — it’s hard to know who you are and to feel like you have a voice of your own,” he said.
“When things started to connect with things like ‘Jealous’ and ‘Chains,’ and those early solo songs, I was relieved because I was like, ‘Oh, I can do this,’” he said

The Jonas Brothers have been touring in celebration of their 20th year together. The brothers performed at MetLife Stadium in East Rutherford, New Jersey, minutes away from where they grew up in Wyckoff, New Jersey.

“To look out and see that sea of people in our hometown stadium was incredible,” Nick Jonas said. “And we took a moment after the show and just kind of looked at each other and said, ‘How did we get here?’ And the answer is: I don’t know, but I’m glad it did.”

title: What to Know About the 2026 NAACP Image Awards, Including How to Watch
summary: When are the NAACP Image Awards? How to watch the ceremony on BET, what time it starts and where it will be streaming
story: The 57th NAACP Image Awards are almost here.

The awards' 57th ceremony is themed "We See You," and it's meant to be a celebration of "Black creativity, excellence, and humanity across every space where our stories are told," NAACP president and CEO Derrick Johnson wrote in a Jan. 11 press release.

Deon Cole will host. The ceremony’s presenters include Halle Bailey, Regé-Jean Page, Chase Infiniti, Delroy Lindo, Janelle James, Miles Caton, Nicole Beharie, Regina Hall, Ryan Coogler, Ryan Michelle Bathé, Sterling K. Brown and more.
Read on for everything to know about the 57th NAACP Image Awards, including who is nominated and how to watch live.
What Day Are the 2026 NAACP Image Awards?
The 57th annual NAACP Image Awards will be on Saturday, Feb. 28, 2026.
The event will be hosted in Los Angeles, California at the Pasadena Civic Auditorium.
What Time Do the 2026 NAACP Image Awards Start?
The 2026 NAACP Image Awards start at 8 p.m. ET/PT.
Where to Watch the 2026 NAACP Image Awards
The 2025 NAACP Image Awards will be broadcast live on BET.
The ceremony will be simulcast across Paramount networks, including CBS, BET HER, CMT, Comedy Central, Logo, MTV, MTV2, PN, Pop, Smithsonian, TV Land, and VH1, per Paramount.
Who Is Nominated for a 2026 NAACP Image Award?
Below, find a few of the notable nominees.

Nominees for this year's entertainer of the year category are Cynthia Erivo, Doechii, Kendrick Lamar, Michael B. Jordon and Teyana Taylor. At 6 total, Teyana Taylor leads nominations across multiple categories, per a NAACP press release. "Sinners" has the most nominations of the motion picture categories, at 18, per the press release. Kendrick Lamar leads the music recording categories, with six nominations, per the press release.
Nominees for this year's entertainer of the year category are Cynthia Erivo, Doechii, Kendrick Lamar, Michael B. Jordon and Teyana Taylor.
At 6 total, Teyana Taylor leads nominations across multiple categories, per a NAACP press release.
"Sinners" has the most nominations of the motion picture categories, at 18, per the press release.
Kendrick Lamar leads the music recording categories, with six nominations, per the press release.
The full list of nominees are available on the NAACP Image Awards website.
Who Is Being Honored at the NAACP Image Awards?
Viola Davis will also be presented with the Chairman’s Award, which honors people who “excel in public service and leverage their unique platforms to ignite and drive meaningful change,” per Variety.
Colman Domingo will receive the President’s Award, which is presented in “recognition of special achievement and distinguished public service,” per Variety.
The ceremony will also induct the group Salt-N-Pepa as well as DJ Spinderella into the NAACP Image Awards Hall of Fame, per Variety.

“As pioneers of hip-hop, Salt-N-Pepa and DJ Spinderella rewrote the rules and boldly claimed space in a genre that forever changed because of their voices, style, and undeniable talent,” NAACP president and CEO Derrick Johnson said. “Inducting them into the NAACP Image Awards Hall of Fame is a celebration of more than an iconic career — it’s a tribute to trailblazers who opened doors, and inspired generations in a way that still resonates today.”
Who Is Being Honored at the NAACP Image Awards Virtual Pre-show?
The awards also feature a virtual award ceremony that was held from Feb. 23 to Feb. 25.
Hosted by actor and writer Angel Laketa Moore and actor and rapper Khleo Thomas, the online ceremony featured three days of awards.

The first day of the awards is available for streaming on YouTube, and featured a win for Michelle Obama, who received a total of five NAACP Image Award Nominations this year, in the outstanding literary work category for her biography "The Look".
On the awards' second day, Cardi B was honored with the outstanding female artist award, along with outstanding album and outstanding hip-hop/rap song, per Variety.
The third and final day of the virtual ceremony focused on the non-televised film, television and writing awards. "Sinners" won the award for outstanding ensemble cast in a motion picture.

title: Ejae Says Her Voice Cracked at the BAFTAS Because She 'Locked Eyes' with this Celebrity: EXCLUSIVE
summary: Ejae says her voice cracked during a performance after locking eyes with Leonardo DiCaprio during an interview with TODAY.
story: Ejae has a “Golden” memory from the 2026 Golden Globes.
The “KPop Demon Hunters” singer joined the 3rd Hour of TODAY on Feb. 27 to reflect on the global impact of her hit song, “Golden.”
EJAE co-wrote “Golden,” which made history as the first K-Pop song to win a Golden Globe in January and a Grammy in February.
She, along with Audrey Nuna and Rei Ami, are the singing voices of the movie’s fictional K-Pop group HUNTER/X.
Despite being the voice of a global sensation, Ejae admitted to an unexpected secret on TODAY: She has "really bad stage fright."
“As a matter of fact, recently I had a performance where I cracked on the first ‘born to be,’ the highest note in the song,” she said.
Before her big performance, Ejae had time to practice, which she recalled as being “so good.”
“It’s unfortunate because during the rehearsals I was amazing,” she said.
This made Ejae feel confident to go out there and “crush this stage.”
Then, right as she was getting to the high note, she made eye contact with a certain celebrity.
“I unfortunately open my eyes and lock eyes with Leonardo DiCaprio,” she said.
Ejae’s voice cracked in that moment. “And I was like, ‘No’!” she exclaimed.
But the show must go on. Ejae persisted and showed the crowd who she was “born to be.”
“I could not but like I had to stay resilient, so I kept true to the song. Like, focus on the lyrics, girl,’’’ she reassured herself.

Ejae didn't specify the awards show, but she recently performed at the BAFTAs where DiCaprio was present and sitting near the stage. Her voice cracked during the first high note. TODAY.com has reached out for comment.
During her TODAY appearance, Ejae spoke to the collaborative work behind the song and the film, noting that “Golden” was the last song written for the movie.
“Golden” is a K-Pop song with Korean and English lyrics. EJAE said that having people from all backgrounds singing the lyrics has been touching.
“Because when I grew up, people didn’t know where Korea was, genuinely,” EJAE shared.
“Now seeing Korea celebrated through this beautiful animated film, it’s just a dream come true,” she added.
In August, “KPop Demon Hunters” officially became the most popular Netflix film to date. The fan-favorite film is not coming to an end, and a confirmed sequel is planned for a 2029 release.

title: Connor Storrie, Michael B. Jordan to Present at the 2026 Actor Awards
summary: Mia Goth, Connor Storie, Michael B. Jordan and Janelle James will present at the 32nd Annual Actor Awards.
story: The 2026 Actor Awards will feature some serious star power.
Mia Goth, Connor Storrie, Michael B. Jordan and Janelle James are among the presenters at the ceremony, formerly known as the Screen Actors Guild Awards.

Storrie is one of the hottest names around, thanks to his work on “Heated Rivalry” and upcoming hosting gig on "Saturday Night Live," while Jordan is nominated at the Actor Awards for outstanding performance by a male actor in a leading role for “Sinners,” which also landed him a nod for outstanding performance by a cast in a motion picture. He’s already won one Actor Award in that category, in 2019 for “Black Panther.”
This year’s field for outstanding performance by a cast in a motion picture also features “Frankenstein,” which stars Goth.

James, meanwhile, is up for outstanding performance by an ensemble in a comedy series for “Abbott Elementary.” This year marks the fourth time James and the hit ABC series have been nominated in that category, but they have never won.

“One Battle After Another” scored a leading seven nominations among films at the Actor Awards. “Sinners” was next, with five, while “Hamnet,” “Marty Supreme” and “Frankenstein” each have three nominations. “The Studio” set the pace on the TV side with five nominations, followed by “Adolescence” and “The White Lotus,” which have four apiece.
Harrison Ford will also receive the SAG-AFTRA Life Achievement Award, the union’s highest honor.
For a complete of nominees in all 15 categories, go here.
The 32nd Annual Actor Awards, featuring Kristen Bell returning as host for the third time, will stream live on Netflix on March 1 at 8 p.m. ET.

title: How 'Grey's Anatomy' Paid Tribute to Eric Dane in a New Episode
summary: Grey's Anatomy pays tribute to Eric Dane with an emotional montage. Find out what's inside and what to do .
story: Following Eric Dane's death, "Grey's Anatomy" is bidding farewell to the late actor and paying tribute to the legacy he left behind on the hit TV series.
Dane, who played the role of Mark “McSteamy” Sloan, died at the age of 53 on Feb. 19. The actor announced his diagnosis with ALS in 2025.
On Feb. 26, the ABC medical drama aired an episode titled "(If You Want) Do It Yourself." A minute-long montage of Dane's time on the show played at the end of the episode.
The tribute was set to Tommee Profitt and Fleurie’s cover of Snow Patrol’s song “Chasing Cars,” which has been played several times throughout the show's 22 seasons.
The minute-long clip began with a scene where Dr. Callie Torres (Sara Ramirez) introduced herself to Dr. Sloan at a bar.
"McSteamy, right?" she asked.
"Mark Sloan," he replied.
The montage then shifted to an awkward moment in Sloan's history: when Dr. Derek Shepherd (Patrick Dempsey) realized that Sloan had slept with Shepherd's wife, Dr. Addison Montgomery (Kate Walsh).
Shepherd and Sloan went on to become friends, and the montage paid tribute to their bromance with several clips.
Sloan, a plastic surgeon, reflected on his career in one scene, saying, "People don't come to me to fix what's on the outside. They come to me to fix what's on the inside."
The montage also highlighted Sloan's friendship with Dr. Torres. Along with Torres and her wife, Arizona (Jessica Capshaw), Sloan helped raise a daughter named Sofia.
Dane's character reflected on the fragility of life in one scene, saying, "Why are you telling me to give it time when there's no guarantee that is going be there?"

In another scene, Sloan offered some romantic advice to a co-star, saying, "If you love someone, you tell them, even if you're scared that it's not the right thing, even if you're scared that it will burn your life to the ground. You say it. You say it loud."
At the end of the tribute, Dane's character uttered an especially poignant line: “Don’t waste one single minute."
Since Dane's death, several of his "Grey's Anatomy" co-stars have paid tribute to him.
Katherine Heigl, who played Dr. Izzie Stevens, shared a message for Dane’s wife, Rebecca Gayheart, and their two daughters.

“All the ‘I’m sorries’ and ‘my prayers are with you’ ring a bit hollow in the midst of so much pain and grief. So I will say the only thing that has ever brought me peace when I’ve lost someone I love,” she wrote.
Heigl then quoted the Robert Frost poem “Nothing Gold Can Stay.”
“I will never forget Eric’s gold and I will forever be grateful I got to bask in it for even a brief moment in time. Thank you for sharing him with us,” she wrote.
Dempsey reflected on Dane’s passing while appearing on Britain’s “The Chris Evans Breakfast Show.”
“Yeah, it’s hard to put into words. I feel really so sad for his children,” he said.
Dempsey also recalled Dane’s “great sense of humor” and said they “got along instantly.”

title: See the 'Love Story' Cast Side-by-Side with the Real People They're Playing
summary: Who's who on Love Story? Find out the truth behind each of the actor and the characters they are playing, plus how they compare to the real people.
story: Casting plays a critical role in any TV show, but it's especially important when you're working on a series that's based on real-life people.

"Love Story," a new FX series that chronicles John F. Kennedy Jr. and Carolyn Bessette-Kennedy’s relationship, is the perfect example of how casting and costumes can bring viewers back to a specific historical moment — and how high the stakes can be.
In June 2025, photos of actor Sarah Pidgeon playing Bessette led some critics to question if "Love Story" would accurately capture the fashion executive's signature style.

Ryan Murphy, the show's writer and executive producer, promptly reassured critics that the photos didn't feature the final costumes and noted that the show had a 12-person "style advisory board" to ensure accuracy.
Naturally, viewers also have strong opinions about the show's cast. After “Love Story” premiered on Feb. 12, many viewers took to Instagram to share their thoughts on the leading lady and man.
"In love with them! Love their chemistry," one social media user commented.
Another weighed in, saying, "Perfect casting! They are extraordinary in their roles."
However, some viewers weren't immediately sold on the actors' performances.
"Not my carolyn not my john," one commented.
Whether you're watching the series or are simply curious, we're taking a closer look at the cast of "Love Story" and the real-life people they're portraying.
Paul Anthony Kelly as John F. Kennedy Jr.
While speaking with The Hollywood Reporter, executive producer and creator Connor Hines revealed that the casting team "saw over 1,000 people" before they found Paul Anthony Kelly.
Hines said JFK Jr. had "an aesthetic that a lot of leading men don’t really have anymore" and a "bygone masculinity."
In an interview with Town & Country, executive producer Nina Jacobson said the team didn't find Kelly until three weeks before the show began filming.
“We weren’t sure if we were going to have to push because we still didn’t have him,” she said.
When the casting team reviewed audition tapes once again, they were impressed by Kelly, a Canadian model.

“Paul was probably one of the first 100, 200 guys we read, and he was always so handsome and there was always something there, but sometimes with casting, you need the right eyes on the right person at the right time for it to fall in place," casting director Nicole Daniels told Town & Country.
After doing a chemistry read with Pidgeon, Kelly was hired.

"Once we had the two of them together, their chemistry, their ease, even though they have very different backgrounds. He was kind of giving up on acting because he didn’t know that it was really going to work, but his modeling was paying off," Jacobson said.
While speaking with Vogue, Kelly explained how he prepared for the role.

"One of the most helpful things, which I would go back to a lot, is that he narrates his father’s book, 'Profiles in Courage.' I’d listen to that every day before set or in between takes or at lunchtime, just to get into his cadence and flow," he said.
Sarah Pidgeon as Carolyn Bessette

In an interview with Town & Country, casting director Nicole Daniels said the show's team was prepared to search for a while to find the right actor to play Carolyn Bessette. But when Sarah Pidgeon auditioned, they knew they'd found the one.

“Carolyn has a more specific look than John did, so we just assumed that it would be harder to find the angular face and all of those things. But Sarah’s tape came in right away and it was pretty clear,” Daniels said.
Pidgeon is normally a brunette, but the team was able to see what she would look like as a blonde by editing a photo of her.

“It did definitely help to get a sense of what she would look like as a blonde. But performance, presence, charisma, mystery, kind of elusiveness, she just brought it all with incredible range. She jumped out and got the part,” Jacobson said.
In an interview with Vogue, Pidgeon said she wasn't surprised that some critics were concerned that those initial photos of her wardrobe didn't accurately represent Bessette's sense of style.

"The online conversations at the beginning of the process served as a reminder of how important it was to get our portrayal of Carolyn correct. She grew even more important to me," she said. "People really, really love Carolyn, and my priority every single day on set was doing justice to her legacy."
Naomi Watts as Jackie Kennedy Onassis
Casting the role of Jackie Kennedy Onassis could've been a massive undertaking, but Murphy knew who he wanted for the character.

"Naomi, that was always Ryan’s thought," Jacobson told Town & Country. "I love her Jackie O. It feels different than anybody else’s you’ve seen, but much more true to people say they recognize the voice, the intonations, but she feels like a mom who loves her kids, who’s like at a different time in her life than we’re used to seeing her. And I think that the complexity, the depth that Naomi was able to bring, it breaks my heart.”
In an interview with The Hollywood Reporter, Hines called Watts "remarkable."

"When I would watch her, I would completely forget that I wrote the show and that I was there to work. She was so mesmerizing in her performance. I felt like I was at a play every day she was on set," Hines said.
Grace Gummer as Caroline Kennedy
