Grace Gummer, the daughter of Meryl Streep, plays the role of JFK Jr.'s sister, Caroline Kennedy.

"We actually never even talked about who her mother was, but Grace also has a relationship with Ryan," Daniels said. "When we were starting our process, she was top of mind for everybody. She won that role, but she had to come and audition, and once she read for us, it was sort of a no-brainer. We were like, this would be crazy not to go with her."
While speaking with L'Officiel, Gummer acknowledged that our society currently has a strong sense of nostalgia for the '90s.

"It reminds us of a time that doesn’t exist anymore — a time before our phones, when life was to be lived and not incessantly documented. Not to be pessimistic, but I feel like it was the end of living freely and moment-to-moment with each other — creatively, artistically, musically. It was also the last time we believed in a collective greater good, politically; the Kennedys were, obviously, the last sort of American royal family that we had to revere and look up to," she said.
Gummer also reflected on the journey her character has taken personally and professionally.

"And I just have an immense amount of respect for her as a mother, an author, a diplomat, an attorney, a sister, a wife, and a daughter. What she has managed to overcome and achieve in her life is beyond admirable. I’m in awe of her," she said.
Alessandro Nivola as Calvin Klein
Carolyn Bessette-Kennedy famously worked for fashion designer Calvin Klein. In "Love Story," Alessandro Nivola stars as Klein and appears in several episodes.
Early on in the series, Bessette helps fit Kennedy for a suit and sparks fly.
Nivola is known for roles in "The Brutalist," "American Hustle" and "Disobedience."
Dree Hemingway as Daryl Hannah
Dree Hemingway, the real-life daughter of actor Mariel Hemingway and the great-granddaughter of Ernest Hemingway, is known for her roles in "Starlet," "While We're Young" and "The Disaster Artist."
In "Love Story," Dree Hemingway plays Daryl Hannah, a famous actor and JFK Jr.'s on-again, off-again girlfriend.
In an interview with The Hollywood Reporter, Dree Hemingway said she doesn't know Hannah personally, but explained that her mother does.

“I reached out (to Hannah). I have no expectations of her reaching out to me in any way, but I basically wrote her a love note of how much I appreciate her as a woman, as an actress, and what an honor of my life this has been,” she said.
Dree Hemingway also shared how she approached the role.

"I mean, I love her so much. It’s an incredible and iconic shoe to fill, but Ryan (Murphy) and Connor Hines, who wrote everything, were so great at characterizing Daryl. The show is definitely told from a fictional aspect because we don’t know what happened (behind closed doors). But it was amazing. She’s amazing," she said.
Leila George as Kelly Klein
Leila George is an Australian actor known for her roles in "Mortal Engines," "Disclaimer" and "He Ain't Heavy."
In "Love Story," George plays the role of Kelly Klein, the former wife of designer Calvin Klein. While reflecting on her experience on set, George shared the following message on Instagram.

"Kelly Klein — by far the best dressed character I’ve ever played!I had SO much fun on this one. The best people!@sarah__pidgeon the coolest chic on screen and off... her talent will blow you away," she wrote. "@alessandro.nivola my partner in crime, the Calvin to my Kelly, I spent most scenes just trying to keep a straight face, he is perfection. Couldn’t have done this one without @williamconacher !! ... @dreelouisehemingway 💕💕 and the rest, so much love for you all xxAnd of course, the beautiful OG @kellyaklein 🤍."
Sydney Lemmon as Lauren Bessette
Lauren Bessette, one of Bessette's two sisters, died in the plane crash that killed Kennedy and Carolyn Bessette in 1999. Lauren Bessette had a twin sister, Lisa Bessette.
After graduating college, Lauren Bessette worked at Morgan Stanley and was later promoted from vice president to a principal, per the New York Times.

Actor Sydney Lemmon, the granddaughter of actor Jack Lemmon, plays Lauren Bessette in "Love Story." Sydney Lemmon has several acting credits to her name, including appearances in the following TV series: "Evil," "Succession," "The Good Fight" and "Fear the Walking Dead."
Constance Zimmer as Ann Messina Freeman
Actor Constance Zimmer plays Bessette's mother, Ann Messina, in "Love Story."
In November 2025, Zimmer took to Instagram to express her gratitude for the experience.

"And there it is in all its glory!! I can finally talk about it! Thank you @deadline @ryanmurphyproductions @connorhines_ @crashbpm and the many more people that brought me here! Having the opportunity to portray this woman, Ann Messina Freeman, was the most emotionally gratifying and heart wrenching person I’ve had the honor of representing on screen. From the script to the cast and crew, this project moved me in so many ways, I’m so beyond grateful to be a part of it!" she wrote.
Zimmer is known for her roles in "UnREAL," "Entourage," "Good Trouble" and "Agents of S.H.I.E.L.D."
Jessica Harper as Ethel Kennedy
Actor Jessica Harper plays the role of Robert F. Kennedy's wife, Ethel Kennedy. She appears in three episodes of "Love Story."
Throughout her life, Ethel Kennedy worked as a human rights advocate.
Harper is known for roles in "Suspiria," "Shock Treatment" and "Minority Report."

title: What Happens in ‘The Traitors’ Season 4 Reunion?
summary: Read a breakdown of the biggest moments from "the Traitors" Season 4 reunion, including Rob and Maura addressing chemistry and Rob revealing he has a girlfriend.
story: Warning: Spoilers for “The Traitors” Season 4 finale and reunion below…
Season 4 of “The Traitors” concluded with a Traitor taking home the entire cash prize for the first time since the show’s inaugural U.S. season.

The finale, out Feb. 26 on Peacock, ended with “Love Island USA” star Rob Rausch carrying out his mischievous plans until the very end, backstabbing all his allies to secure the $220,800 prize pot for himself. It was an impressive feat that only one other Traitor, Season 1 star Cirie Fields, has achieved. (Read TODAY.com’s recap of the epic conclusion here.)

Rob said in the finale that his multiple betrayals, which included cutting fellow Traitor Eric Nam and taking all the money from Maura Higgins, would all be worth it if he won. But in the reunion, which was also released Feb. 26, he had to face the music.

All the Season 4 stars returned to unpack the season in a fiery reunion hosted by Andy Cohen. Throughout the episode, Rob was called out for his actions in the castle. Tension also remained between multiple other players, like Lisa Rinna and Colton Underwood, leading to some must-see confrontations.
Read on for a breakdown of the biggest moments from the reunion.
The Traitors Reunite
All the Traitors reunite to join Andy on the reunion set. Rob humorously downplays his victory, saying he “got lucky.”

Fellow traitor Candiace Dillard Bassett agrees that luck played a role, as well as smarts. “I think this group of Faithfuls need to go to faithful school and perhaps that impacted some of the success,” Candiace says.
Eric says he has gotten over the betrayal. Donna “Mama” Kelce compliments Rob’s strategy.
The conversation switches to Colton. Candiace says Rob used Colton until he was dispensable. Rob doesn’t deny that he did whatever it took to win.
Donna Kelce Reflects on Being a Traitor

Donna Kelce made history this season as the first Secret Traitor. Despite her quick exit, Donna says she had a “blast.” The other Traitors praise her poker face when she found out host Alan Cumming had selected her for the role.
After sharing that her sons, Travis and Jason Kelce, were proud and impressed with her gameplay, Andy asks if future daughter-in-law Taylor Swift watched this season.
“Yes, she’s a watcher,” Donna replies. “So is her family. They love ‘Traitors.’”
Rob Defends Himself for Turning on Lisa and Candiace
Rob tells Andy that instantly when he saw Lisa and Candiace were the other two Traitors, he assumed they would eventually turn on him.
But the ladies push back, pointing out that Rob jumped to conclusions and betrayed them first.
Rob says “the ship was sinking” which made him have to go with the majority and vote for Lisa.
“I needed to be on the right side of history,” Rob adds. He says he wanted to ensure his voting record wouldn’t be questioned later on.
Candiace says that looking back on her experience, she suspects that Rob was feeding Colton information about the Traitors.
“That’s crazy,” Rob retorts.
Candiace reveals that Colton shared information with her that she will reveal when he joins them on stage.
“You literally wanted to go to the end by yourself and I would respect you more if you said that,” Candiace says to Rob. He denies this.
Candiace says she planted seeds about Rob with her votes before she left. He points out that it didn’t work. He also says he never trusted Candiace.
Maura Addresses Chemistry with Rob
Maura says she was “genuinely shocked” when Rob confirmed he was a Traitor. She adds that watching the show back she felt “embarrassed” for instantly trusting Rob.
Andy notes the “undeniable chemistry” between Maura and Rob. But she says they were “genuinely just friends.”
“We never, like, had a flirt,” she says, which earns a skeptical look from Rob. But he agrees they are just friends.
He also reveals he has had a girlfriend for “almost two months.”
Candiace Says Colton Told Her Rob Confirmed He Was a Traitor

Candiace reveals that her secret is that Rob confirmed her identity to Colton when they were in the castle. Rob says this isn’t possible because there are strict rules all the Traitors have to follow.
“I was in an excellent spot. Why would I risk it and tell Colton I’m a Traitor?” Rob responds.
But Colton does say that Rob threw out Candiace’s name as the next to go after Lisa. Rob does not recall mentioning Candiace’s name first. “I would never cheat in this game,” Colton says.
Lisa Calls Out Colton For His ‘Hostage’ Comment
Lisa asks Colton to explain why he said in a previous episode he was going to hold her “hostage” when he approached her about being a Traitor.

“I think the whole premise of it is when you find out that you find a Traitor, you are supposed to murder them and get them out,” he explains. He says his plan was to keep her around and see if she would help him eliminate another Traitor.
“I think it was an interesting word to use,” Lisa replies.
Candiace agrees. “Maybe the wrong word,” she chimes in.
Colton says Lisa also said in the turret she wanted to “slit my f—- throat.”
Lisa acknowledges she said that. “I can look at you any way I want and I can say whatever I want, actually,” she adds.
Colton then addresses the overwhelming criticism he has received since the show began.

“I think what I can understand from people viewing me back on television, if you didn’t follow my story and what I went through and you watched me as the ‘Bachelor’ in 2019 and then all of a sudden you’re tuning in in 2026 — I had an incredibly difficult couple of years,” Colton says. “I’ve grown and I’ve changed and I’ve put so much work into myself and into my family. I never really thought the way that I would show up and play ‘Traitors’ would be linked to one of the most darkest and most horrible moments of my life.”
He said he can understand why people are talking about it.
Porsha Williams and Michael Rapaport Go Head-to-Head
Michael Rapaport defends his chaotic gameplay. He says he knew Porsha Williams wasn’t a Traitor but he continued to push for her to go because his “integrity was on the line.”
Porsha replies, “Michael, you did not have any integrity in that moment.” She adds that she feels like he used her.
“You need to figure out a way to use your words … when I’m talking, you’re not talking,” Michael screams as a back-and-forth begins.
Finally, Andy has to step in to shut down the argument.
Michael Speaks About His Colton Round Table Comment

Andy brings up the controversial comment Michael made at a Round Table about Colton being able to keep a secret, which led some viewers to believe it was a reference to the former “Bachelor” star’s sexuality.

“I feel really bad that it got to that point. I feel really bad that I antagonized Colton,” Michael says. “I reached out to him. As much as I like to talk trash, I’m not a person that likes to be respectful or hurt feelings.”
Colton says he had planned to keep the private conversation with Michael between them. He says anything that wasn’t game related “was off limits” for him.

title: 'Love Story': The True Story of JFK Jr. and Carolyn Bessette's Explosive Fight in a New York Park
summary: Why did JFK Jr and Carolyn Bessette get in a fight in a Manhattan park?
story: A new FX series series titled “Love Story” explores the highs and lows of John F. Kennedy Jr. and Carolyn Bessette's relationship.
The couple, who died in a plane crash in 1999 along with Bessette's sister Lauren Bessette, captivated the world with their whirlwind romance, which was covered extensively in tabloids at the time.
The fifth episode, titled "Battery Park," depicts the couple's alleged fight in a New York City park in 1996. What happened between them, and how was it covered in the media at the time?
Here's what to know.
Did JFK Jr. and Carolyn Bessette Really Fight at Battery Park?
In Bessette’s biography “Once Upon a Time,” which inspired the FX series, author Elizabeth Beller offers insight into the fight that caused a stir in the tabloids.
Per Beller, Bessette and Kennedy did indeed have a very public argument in February 1996. The disagreement occurred after the couple had brunch at the Tribeca Grill, per Beller's book.
“First, they argued in front of the restaurant, both making wild gestures and obviously screaming at each other. John’s face was full of fury, and Carolyn gave it right back to him,” she wrote.
Publications at the time reported that the couple fought in Battery Park, but Beller’s book shares a different description.

“They went home to get their dog, Friday, and completely unaware their every move was being captured on camera by paparazzo Angie Coqueran, brought him to Washington Square Park, where they continued their quarrel,” she wrote.
During the argument, Kennedy pulled Bessette’s engagement ring off of her finger.

“They were both crying, pushing each other, and yelling at the top of their lungs. John stormed off, and Carolyn ran after him, not one to stand for being left in the lurch. When she reached him, she leapt on his back. He turned around, shoved her off, and then they yelled more, close up in each other’s faces,” Beller wrote.
After stopping to sit on a park bench for a few minutes, the couple continued walking and “were at it again after a few blocks.”
“Finally, John just sat down on the curb, buried his head in his arms, and cried. Carolyn was pacing back and forth, and then offered her hand, and John put the ring back on,” Beller wrote.
What Were John and Carolyn Fighting About?

In the show, Carolyn and John fight over his remarks at the unveiling of George Magazine. He denied, both in a statement and brief remarks, that they were engaged. This sparks a blowout — both that John spoke about their private lives and that what he said was untrue.
The real cause, it seems, is different.
In her book, Beller notes that many news outlets incorrectly reported what caused the argument, saying it was about “Carolyn’s jealousy over John flirting with another woman.”
“Some reports had the other woman as Sharon Stone, some claimed it was O.J. Simpson’s girlfriend Paula Barbieri, and some didn’t name anyone specifically but noted John’s ‘roving eye,’” she wrote.
Beller explained that the fight was actually a result of “Carolyn’s anger that John was letting himself be exploited” and letting others take advantage of him.

Beller said Steve Gillon, one of Kennedy's friends, told her about an incident that had taken place at a wedding which caused conflict. Bessette felt the bride was using Kennedy's presence to get the New York Times to cover her wedding, and was upset he didn't "make a statement by walking out."
What Happened After the Fight?
Unbeknownst to the couple, their argument was captured on video.

“‘Fights, Camera, Action’ was the headline George Rush ran in the Daily News when he reported that a weekly television tabloid show called Day & Date, on CBS, had won the bidding war for the tape and would be broadcasting the entirety of the fight in living color over five days beginning on Monday, March 11,” Beller wrote.
The author also described how the National Enquirer's Steve Coz initially bought the tape and photos and told Newsweek the bidding war for the tape was "in the $100,000 range."
"Soon enough, more photos of the fight appeared. Each report was worse, more salacious, more vile than the last," Beller wrote.

Both Kennedy and Bessette faced backlash for their public fight, but Beller noted that Bessette got the brunt of the public's criticism. She was "captured ... in one of her worst moments," according to the book.
The coverage had ramifications on her life: Soon after, she left her job at Calvin Klein.

"It had become increasingly impossible for her to walk into the office due to the stalking paparazzi's presence. With the chaos of the press tracking her every move, perhaps even taking attention away from the brand, it no longer made sense to stay on," she wrote.
Bessette and Kennedy married in 1996 and died in a plane crash, along with Bessette's sister Lauren Bessette, in 1999.

title: 'Love Story': When Did JFK Jr. Propose and How Long Did it Take Carolyn Bessette to Say Yes?
summary: When did JFK Jr propose to Carolyn Bessette? How long did it take her to say yes? Why was she worried?
story: Episode 5 of “Love Story” reaches a pivotal turning point in John F. Kennedy Jr. and Carolyn Bessette’s relationship: A proposal.

In the show, JFK Jr. (played by Paul Kelly Anthony) pops the question during a very tense weekend with his family. Carolyn (Sarah Pidgeon) feels clearly ostracized and out of place, especially after Ethel Kennedy’s judgmental glances and she — gasp — forgets to sign up for breakfast.
John, however, doesn't seem to notice and chooses a moment while they're fishing to propose.
“The thing about fishing is, it’s always so much better with a partner,” he says. Then he takes out a silver sapphire band. He said he’d been waiting for a “perfect moment.”

Her response is warm but not conclusive. Perhaps the sting of Ethel’s grilling is still hurting. “I love you. I do want to be with you. But there’s a lot of big stuff we have to talk about before we can get married. Like, how our lives really fit together,” she says.
"Is that a no?" he responds.
"I think we need to have more of a conversation. This way I can really mean it when I say yes. Which I think is what we both want," she says.
The rest of their episode shows Carolyn inching closer to yes. They have another conversation weeks later where she expresses her concerns about becoming Mrs. JFK Jr.
"This will radically change my life," she says. John promises his personal life will be "off limits to the public" but she's skeptical. He promises to protect her.
She accepts the ring, which was his mom's, but says she'll only wear it when it's the two of them.
Somehow, though, the moment becomes the world's — it makes the tabloids' front page.
Carolyn says yes only after they have a massive fight, which leads to another breakthrough. John essentially promises he won't force her into family vacations.

"I don't want to change you. I don't want to bring you to my world. I want you to pull me out of it. I want you to be my family. You and me on our own private island even when we're in a crowded room," he says.
She stresses that she wants a simple life — not first lady. "Let's not ignore the reality of who you are and who your family is," she says.
He swears he wants to be a "good man," not a "great man," and just a good partner. That's enough.
"To be honest, I don't think marriage is necessary," she says, smoking a cigarette. "But I'm down to do it, with you."
She starts crying, realizing the weight of it. The episode ends with Carolyn crying and saying, "Yes."
"Yes, I"ll marry you. I'll take the risk with you and we'll drive each other crazy, for as long as we both shall live," she says, as The Goo Goo Dolls' "Name" plays.
What Happened in Real Life?
In real life, Carolyn Bessette-Kennedy and John F. Kennedy Jr. met in 1992 and dated briefly, before reconnecting in 1993 and getting engaged in 1995.
The show plays around with the timeline but keeps the facts the same: Bessette, indeed, wanted to keep the engagement a secret.

According to “Fairy Tale Interrupted,” the 2012 biography written by RoseMarie Terenzio, Kennedy’s assistant at George who also knew Bessette well, Kennedy proposed on the Fourth of July weekend at Martha’s Vineyard in 1995.
Kennedy took Bessette out fishing and said, "Fishing is so much better with a partner," presenting her with the same type of ring seen in the show — diamonds and sapphires.
Terenzio said Bessette told her she "didn't want anyone to know" after he proposed, according to the book.
"Carolyn was worried that when their engagement went public, the media would tear into her," Terenzio wrote.
Kennedy’s friend Robert Littell wrote in his book, “The Men We Became,” that Bessette “resisted his proposal for a year.”

Terenzio said as the summer went on, Bessette “loosened up” when it came to wearing the ring. But the Friday before Labor Day, the New York Post ran a story with a closeup of the ring, sparking rumors.
The famous fight, which was photographed and recorded, happened six months after the proposal, in February 1996.
There is no indication that Bessette finally accepted his proposal after the fight like what happens in the show.
Speaking to TODAY.com, Bessette's biographer Elizabeth Beller reminded viewers that the show is a dramatization, not a documentary.

title: Carolyn Bessette-Kennedy's Sapphire Engagement Ring Was Inspired by Jackie Onassis
summary: Since the premiere of “Love Story” on FX and Hulu, people have been celebrating Carolyn Bessette-Kennedy’s iconic style
story: Since the premiere of “Love Story” on FX and Hulu, people have been celebrating Carolyn Bessette-Kennedy’s iconic style.
The fifth episode, "Battery Park," zeroes in on a specific piece of her wardrobe: Her engagement ring.

John F. Kennedy Jr., played by Paul Kelly Anthony, proposes while the two are on an intense weekend with the entire Kennedy clan. Carolyn is unable to say yes due to various concerns, which the episode unpacks.
But she does keep coming back to the ring.
According to Elizabeth Beller, author of the Bessette biography "Once Upon a Time," the ring tied together both Kennedy's family history and Bessette's style.

Kennedy modeled the ring off of what his mother, Jacqueline Kennedy Onassis, called her “swimming ring”: “pared down and elegant, perfect for swimming.” (Her first engagement ring was a combined five carats of diamonds and emeralds.)
Kennedy Onassis’ ring was made with emerald and diamonds and set in gold, whereas Bessette’s was platinum with sapphires and diamonds.
The ring “was perfect for Carolyn and her understated (elegance)” Beller writes. The fashion executive rarely wore jewelry and was known for her simple, classic looks.
Bessette never met Kennedy’s mother, who died of cancer in 1994.

This was a point of contention for the couple, who met in 1993. Beller quoted an anonymous friend of Bessette’s who said Bessette broke up with Kennedy “a couple of times” because he wouldn’t introduce her to his mother. Once, at Keens Steakhouse, “John rushed Carolyn out a side door when he spied Jackie across the room with her companion.”

Beller quotes Kennedy’s friend Billy Way, who said Kennedy “regretted” never introducing Bessette to his mother and pointed out their similarities — both children of divorced parents, both Catholic and both with a minimalist sense of style.
Bessette and Kennedy were married in 1995 in a small, secret ceremony on Cumberland Island in Georgia.
Speaking to TODAY.com, jeweler Page Neal, co-founder of Bario Neal, calls Bessette’s ring a “study in intentional simplicity.”

“At a time when engagement rings often centered on display and visibility, hers reflected a more personal and quietly confident point of view. Its restraint is precisely what gives it lasting power,” Neal said.
The ring, Neal says, fits into Bessette’s minimal, refined and “deeply assured” style.

“She favored clean lines and understated silhouettes, outfits that never feel overworked. This ring mirrors that sensibility. An eternity band sits close to the hand so that it becomes part of the wearer, rather than something that announces itself. That subtle integration is what makes it feel so aligned with her style,” Neal says.

Unlike many modern engagement rings, this was an eternity band, meaning there was no significant center stone (usually a diamond). The idea of a “swimming ring” suggests a piece “meant to be lived in, not taken off at the first sign of activity.”
“A ring like Carolyn’s doesn’t depend on scale to feel meaningful. Its appeal is rooted in proportion, personal expression and wearability,” Neal says.

title: ‘The Traitors’ Season 4 Finale Recap: Who Won It All?
summary: Find out if the Faithfuls took home the gold, or if the Traitors stole it for themselves in "The Traitors" Season 4 finale.
story: Warning: Spoilers for “The Traitors” Season 4 finale below.
After weeks of backstabbing, tension-filled Round Tables and devious gameplay, “The Traitors” Season 4 has come to an end.

Entering the finale, which arrived on Peacock Feb. 26, only six players remained: “Love Island USA” star Rob Rausch (Traitor), singer Eric Nam (Traitor), “Dancing With the Stars” pro Mark Ballas (Faithful), “Love Island” star Maura Higgins (Faithful) and Olympic figure skaters Johnny Weir (Faithful) and Tara Lipinski (Faithful). The Traitors could see the finish line, with a pot of gold waiting for them at the end. But, they had to outmaneuver the remaining Faithfuls for one last time.

Did they make it to the Fire of Truth and win the game? Or did the Faithfuls finally work together to banish the remaining Traitors? Find out the identity of the last player (or players) standing, below.
The Final Murder

Host Alan Cumming decides to pull out all the stops for the final, public murder of the season. When the remaining players join him in front of the castle, he reveals that Mark has been murdered by the Traitors.
“See you in hell,” Mark says before lying down in a coffin.
“Slowly, death comes for us all,” Alan says as he takes a torch and lights a ring of fire, surrounding the coffin in flames. “Ashes to ashes, dust to dust.”
In his final confessional, Mark is disappointed but content with his sendoff.

“It sucks. I thought I was going to make it all the way,” he says. “But, I think I was murdered because I was kind of closing in on whoever the Traitors are and I would probably put my money on Eric right now. I’m deeply disappointed I didn’t get to take my final shot. But I did go out in a blaze of glory.”
Tara and Johnny’s Hail Mary

At breakfast, Eric and Rob both reflect on how difficult the journey to the end of the game has been. They also agree that they need to spend the day convincing Maura to vote out Johnny at the next Round Table.
Alan reminds the players that no one’s true identity will be revealed after the remaining banishments before the group starts to prepare for their last mission.

Johnny is sticking to his theory that he pitched last week: Eric and Rob are both Traitors. He pulls Tara aside and they realize that they have to bring Maura to their side or else they will be outnumbered at the next Round Table. Meanwhile, Eric and Rob are already busy smooth-talking Maura.

Tara interrupts their conversation to have a private chat with Maura. She tells her that she needs to vote for Eric to be banished next. Maura listens to her and acknowledges in her confessional that Tara’s reasoning makes sense.
The Players Make a Splash

Alan saves his most chilling and daring mission for last. The players meet him in front of the castle and he shares that all the eliminated players could be their “key to success.” He then asks for two volunteers before a helicopter flies over the castle grounds.

Rob and Maura volunteer and are given a chest that they have to hold while riding in the helicopter. Meanwhile, the rest of the players head to the loch. While seated on the helicopter, Maura tells Rob that they need to eliminate Eric next, making him worry that his plan will go awry.

The other three players steer a boat to different pontoons resting in the loch. The pontoons have questions regarding the departed players. The chest Maura and Rob hold contains key cards with all the players’ faces. They must drop the card that answers the corresponding question into the loch. Tara, Eric and Johnny then have to retrieve the card and attach it to the pontoon. If they answer all the questions correctly. They add $20,000 to the prize pot.

Both groups successfully work together and deliver the right answers. But Alan, as always, has a twist to reveal. He pauses the mission clock and says the group can double their money. Beside him is a chest with an extra $20,000. Either Maura or Rob has to jump out of the helicopter, swim to a buoy, snag the card attached to it and reach Alan before the remaining time on the clock runs out.
Rob volunteers since Maura cannot swim and completes the task, bringing the final total of the prize pot to $220,800.
The Last Round Table
Back at the castle, Johnny and Tara try, again, to persuade Maura to join their side. Maura says the game has become “messy” and she is starting to doubt herself.

When the Round Table begins, Eric speaks up for the first time in multiple episodes. He announces that he is leaning toward Johnny or Tara. They counter by saying that Eric has been quiet up until this point, which makes him look suspicious. Eric defends himself and says he has been working with and confiding in the players he trusts. Tara calls him out for throwing Maura under the bus earlier, but Rob jumps in to also share the theory that Johnny and Tara are Traitors.
The votes are split, with two for Johnny and two for Eric. Maura has the deciding vote and she banishes Johnny. Before he leaves, he gives bestie Tara a hug.

“Of course it’s not nice to lose, but I’m so proud of what I’ve done here, and I was able to do it with Tara by my side,” Johnny says in his departing confessional. “Eric and Rob are the Traitors and I hope for Tara’s sake that maybe Maura sees something that she hasn’t seen yet.”
The Fire of Truth

After the Round Table, Tara is feeling defeated. Elsewhere, Rob tells Maura she needs to eliminate Tara next. In return, Maura makes him promise that they will also eliminate Eric, who she is now fully convinced is a Traitor. Tara also makes one last ditch effort by repeatedly telling Maura that Eric and Rob are Traitors and shouldn’t be trusted.

The end is near, but first Alan chats with the players to see how they are feeling before facing the Fire of Truth. Eric is feeling nervous but has faith in his allies. Tara wants to win for herself and Johnny. Rob says all his shady moves throughout the season will be worth it if he wins. He also reveals he wants to use the money to help his family and start planning for his future family. Maura worries about putting all her trust in Rob after previously mentioning that she has been cheated on repeatedly and doesn’t trust easily.

For the Fire of Truth, each player is handed two pouches: one that says “End Game” (which is represented by green smoke) and one that says “Banish Again” (which is represented by red smoke).” The game automatically ends if only two players are remaining.
For the first vote, all the players choose to banish again. Tara voices her distrust of Eric and asks Maura to support her. But, Maura sides with Eric and Rob, again, and Tara is eliminated.

“I am feeling so devastated,” Tara says in her confessional. “When I went to Maura, I thought she realized by what I was telling her that it’s Eric and Rob. But I never in a million years thought I’d make it this far. I did the very best I could in that room and to me that’s priceless.”

During the second vote, Eric opts to end the game but Maura and Rob choose to banish again. A stunned Eric says that he is hurt that the two betrayed them. He tries to convince Maura to doubt Rob, but it doesn’t work. Maura and Rob banish Eric, with Rob telling his fellow Traitor that he had to keep the promise he made to Maura.

In his final confessional, Eric is in disbelief. “Wow. Wow.  If I’d done that,  I just wouldn’t be able to live with myself in that way,” he says about Rob’s betrayal. “Money’s great, but I think relationships are more important and it doesn’t feel good.”
Maura is smiling wide, not knowing that Rob has one more secret to confess. She proudly says she is a Faithful and waits for him to say the same.
“Maura, I am, and I always have been, a Traitor,” he reveals.

Maura is speechless and stares at Rob in silence while he repeatedly apologizes. “Oh my God. I’m so stupid. I was so fooled by you. You absolute a------. You pinky promised. You’re a piece of work,” she tells him.”
She adds, “I was so convinced. You’ve embarrassed me.”
When he tries to praise her for being the last Faithful she replies, “Don’t speak. Please stop talking.”
Maura’s final confessional begins with her just shaking her head. “Oh dear God. I’m a fool,” she laughs. “This is why I don’t trust anybody ever. Because they stab you in the g—- back.”

She tells Rob, “You’re never going to have a girlfriend after this. You’re such a good liar.” But, she isn’t bitter. “Fair play though. If I was a Traitor, I would’ve done the same. No hard feelings. I think I’m just in shock. I actually thought I was winning,” she says.
Her last words this season: “He’s a bad man. He’s a really bad, bad man. But, good for him. Rob, you’re a f------ snake. But well done.”
The Traitor Takes It All

As the last player remaining, Rob shares with Alan that he is feeling mixed emotions. “I’ve won, but I’ve also betrayed the person that I was closest to in the game,” he says before Alan points out he was playing a game called “The Traitors.”

“It’s so bittersweet,” Rob says in his confessional. “I feel bad for Maura. Poor Eric, too. But it was an opportunity that was right in front of me. Would you give $100,000 to someone you met three weeks ago? I wouldn’t.”

Alan and Rob go outside and he relishes in being the sole winner. “I could make this money go a long way. This will change my life and my family’s life. I had to think with that emotion, like I’ve done this whole game,” he shares.
As fireworks are set off behind him and he celebrates his victory, Robs marvels, “People just see me as a dumb, hot person. But, I’ve been a Traitor from day one, and I won. Beat everyone.”
What’s next for him? Well, he says he is ready to “find a nice girl that hasn’t watched TV too much,” and buy a farm where he can raise a family. “I want to have a quiet life,” he says.

title: Pink Denies She and Husband Carey Hart Separated: 'Don’t Believe the Hype'
summary: Pink is setting the record straight after reports surfaced that she and husband Carey Hart had separated.
story: Pink is setting the record straight after reports surfaced that she and husband Carey Hart had separated.

The “Cover Me in Sunshine” singer posted an Instagram video on Feb. 26 denying the rumors and writing in the caption, “Like I always say. If you don’t hear it from me, don’t believe the hype. Stay tuned though! Who knows what could happen next!?!”
In the selfie video, which contains strong language, Pink calls out the outlets that reported the separation.
“So, I was just alerted to the fact that I’m separated from my husband. I didn’t know,” she says, naming the publications and jokingly thanking them for “letting me know.”
“I was wondering, would you also like to tell our children, my 14-year-old and 9-year-old are also unaware,” she said. “Or do you want to talk about some real news?”
Instead, Pink suggested talking about the Epstein files, systemic racism or misogyny in sports, among other topics.

“Or how classy the women’s hockey team is, or how eight of the 12 medals won in the Olympics this year for the US were won by women,” she continued. “Or do you maybe want to talk about the fact that I got nominated the first year I was eligible for the Rock and Roll m------------ Hall of Fame? Do you want to talk about my accomplishments, or do you only want to talk about my supposed demise?”
Pink called the reports “fake news, not true,” before telling her followers that she loves them and “go with God.”
She ended her message by telling “trash news” to “do better.”

Pink and the former motocross racer have been married for 20 years. They first met in 2001 at the X Games and got married in 2006. Pink and Hart share two children together, daughter Willow, 14, and son Jameson, 9.
The couple, however, has broken up before. Once in 2003 before they got engaged two years later, and another time in 2008. They got back together a year later.
When asked about reconciling in a February 2013 interview with Redbook, Pink said, “People are always like, ‘Why did you get back together?’ Well, we weren’t done.”
At the time, she said that 11 months after they broke up, Hart invited her to perform at his Las Vegas club on New Year's Eve. She then gave him a photo album with cards he had given her.

“On the last page, I pasted a photo of me from a really bad movie I made years ago with my neck slit and blood everywhere,” she said. “Next to it I wrote, ‘This is me without you.’ On the next page, there was a picture of a baby. And I wrote: ‘The rest is unwritten.’ The divorce papers that we never signed were behind that page. I was like, ‘The rest is up to you.’”
She also told the Los Angeles Times in October 2023 that her song “So What” was part of what got them together.
Over the years, Pink has been candid about her 20-year marriage, telling The Guardian in 2019, “Monogamy is work! But you do the work and it’s good again.”

title: How the Ending to 'Bridgerton' Season 4 Tees up Stories for 2 Characters
summary: Find out how Season 4, Part 2 wrapped up Benedict and Sophie's Cinderella story, and learn how Francesca and Eloise's stories might unfold in Seasons 5 and 6.
story: Warning: This story contains major spoilers for the ending of "Bridgerton" Season 4 and the plots of Julia Quinn's "To Sir Phillip, With Love" and "When He Was Wicked."
Another social season has come to an end in Mayfair, and Luke Thompson's Benedict Bridgerton and Yerin Ha's Sophie Baek — sorry, Gun — have found their happily ever after.
But based on Julia Quinn's "Bridgerton" novels that inspired the hit Netflix drama, we're only halfway through the family's saga.

The illustrious Bridgerton family consists of eight alphabetically-named children, and four have yet to get their own season: Eloise (Claudia Jessie), Francesca (Hannah Dodd), Gregory (Will Tilston) and Hyacinth (Florence Hunt).

"Bridgerton" showrunner Jess Brownell previously confirmed to TODAY.com that each family member will get their own starring season of the show. So who's up next in the already renewed Seasons 5 and 6?

As Deadline reported, Brownell revealed at the red carpet for Part 1 that Eloise and Francesca will be the leads of the two upcoming seasons, though she didn't say which season goes with which character. However, some of the final moments of Season 4 are filled with clues about the storyline for next season.
But first, let's recap how Season 4, Part 2, which dropped on Netflix Feb. 26, wrapped up Benedict and Sophie's Cinderella story.
How Does 'Bridgerton' Season 4 End?
Another happy ending!
"Bridgerton" Season 4 concludes with Benedict and Sophie getting together, despite her illegitimate status and her work as a maid.

After she initially rejected his offer to be his mistress, Sophie and Benedict sleep together for the first time after he says he wants to commit to her and professes his love. Their relationship status continues to go undefined until Benedict finally wins mom Violet's (Ruth Gemmell) approval, who gives her blessing for them to quietly live together in the countryside.

But Sophie's stepmother, Araminta (Katie Leung), wants vengeance for Sophie's night out as the lady in silver. She finally tracks Sophie down and has her arrested, alleging she stole the Dowager Countess of Penwood's shoe clips.

Through a series of societal machinations, like the details of a hidden will and Violet pulling rank, the Bridgertons are able to strike a deal with Araminta. In exchange for not publicly revealing that Araminta stole the dowry willed to Sophie by her late father, Sophie will be introduced into society as a Gun and as a distant relative of the late Earl of Penwood, allowing her to be accepted by the nobility and make her an "acceptable" match for Benedict.

Queen Charlotte (Golda Rosheuvel) sweeps in at the last second to unearth all the juicy gossip involving the two families and to approve the latest Bridgerton match. Benedict later proposes publicly on the dance floor.
Who Will Lead Season 5 of 'Bridgerton'?
Season 5, which was renewed back in May 2025, will be led by either Eloise or Francesca, and the ending of "Bridgerton" Season 4 leaves potential for both stories.

Francesca, after having candid conversations with husband John, the Duke of Kilmartin (Victor Alli), finally found herself feeling more steady in their relationship. Though the surprise arrival of his cousin, Michaela (Masali Baduza), threw her off kilter again.
But Francesca and Michaela broke the ice after a party, and the trio developed a delightful friendship. However, in a shocking ending to Episode 6, Francesca finds John dead in his bed.
As she mourns, she finds herself leaning on Michaela. One night, she asks Michaela to stay in London with her amid the search for the heir to the Kilmartin line.
"If you would like it, I shall stay," Michaela says with a smile.
"I feel very close to you, Michaela," Francesca responds as she grabs Michaela's hand.
Michaela, though, looks tightly at their conjoined hands and pulls away quickly. After that slightly tense moment, Michaela suddenly departs London.
"She has packed her belongings and left," a maid tells a horrified Francesca.

As for Eloise, the most studious Bridgerton is, again, mainly stuck in the shadows this season, dodging eligible bachelors and teasing young sister Hyacinth for her dreams of the marriage mart. But by the end of the season, it's clear Eloise is starting to change her tune.
She apologizes to Hyacinth for mocking her and reveals that the death of John reminds her to "ensure that we do not allow fear to keep us from experiencing something that could be truly special."
Eloise clarifies while she’s not an outright "proponent" of marriage, she can see that "on occasion, marriage might have its advantages."

During the ball in the final episode, she finds herself in the unexpected role of matchmaker, encouraging Sophie's half-sister Posy (Isabella Wei), to dance with the handsome suitor she is eyeing. Later, Eloise smiles to herself as she sees the two talking.
What Happens in Francesca’s Book, ‘When He Was Wicked'?
The sixth book in the "Bridgerton" series, "When He Was Wicked," picks up with the events seen at the end of Season 4: John's death.
The book opens with Francesca living a life of domestic tranquility, truly in love and close friends with her husband's cousin, Michael.

After John's death, Michael disappears for a time, shirking the responsibilities of becoming the Earl of Kilmartin. When he returns, he brings a shocking confession with him: he has always been in love with Francesca.
Their story follows the push and pull between Michael and Francesca as they each navigate grief and an undeniable attraction.

In the show adaptation, Francesca is being set up for a queer spin on that tale, with Michaela replacing Michael in the story. Hints at their eventual romance were dropped as early as the Season 3 finale. Though Francesca and John were already happily married, Francesca quite literally was at a loss for words when she met Michaela for the first time, barely able to state her own name.

Michaela's romantic background, meanwhile, has not been disclosed yet in the show. But during Season 4, Episode 5, she storms out of the room after Francesca seemingly tries to set her up with a male suitor.
"I asked you kindly not to force suitors onto Michaela," John scolds Francesca after.
While they make amends, Michaela explains, "There are very few people I feel secure with, and John is one of them."
What Happens in Eloise's Book, 'To Sir Phillip, With Love'?

Like her sister, viewers have already been introduced to Eloise's love interest from the book. The novel that follows her love story is titled "To Sir Phillip, With Love," which is the fifth book in the "Bridgerton" series.

Viewers met the show's version of Phillip years ago when Season 1 debuted in 2020. Phillip (Chris Fulton) was the brother of a man that Marina (Ruby Barker), the mysterious Featherington cousin who was hiding a secret, loved. In the Season 1 finale, Marina accepted Phillip's marriage proposal and they left London together. The couple briefly appeared in Season 2, Episode 4 when Colin Bridgerton (Luke Newton) visited them.
Marina and Phillip haven't popped up on the show since then.
However, based on the book, Phillip will be re-introduced after Marina dies. He begins corresponding with Eloise, who expresses her condolences, and they later develop feelings for each other.

title: Trump's MAGA base wrestles with Iran strikes launched by an 'America First' president
summary: During his campaigns for president, Trump pledged to focus on domestic issues and end foreign wars — a promise that some see him not upholding.
story: Some of President Donald Trump’s biggest political supporters say they are feeling abandoned after the U.S. joined Israel in bombing Iran, an operation that was launched without congressional approval and has so far left six U.S. service members dead.

Trump officials acknowledge “Operation Epic Fury,” as it’s formally named, comes with political risk, but they argue that the military operation will be short term and is in line with his “peace through strength” campaign pledge.

“We understand some will reflexively oppose this, people don’t love the idea of war,” said a Trump adviser who, like others in this piece, was granted anonymity to speak candidly. “But people like winners. They are drawn to it. If we do this quickly, I think it will be seen as what it is: successful.”

But the war is a stark departure from Trump’s long-held “America First” campaign pledge, which was predicated on the idea of focusing on domestic issues rather than foreign entanglements. Trump has indicated that there is no end date to the operation in Iran, which risks spiraling into a wider regional conflict.
For the MAGA base, the abrupt bombing of Iran seems like a drastic departure from one of Trump’s core policy promises, and one that could hurt him and Republicans in the key 2026 midterms.

I “don’t see how this helps Republicans in tight races across the country,” Curt Mills, executive director of The American Conservative magazine, said. “In fact, I think it may orient against them. This turns off a lot of independents.”
Former Fox News host and ardent Trump supporter Megyn Kelly said Monday she has “serious doubts” about the Iran strike, which drew a direct rebuke from Trump.
Kelly “was opposed to me for years when I ran the first time and nothing stopped me,” Trump said in an interview with The Inner Circle newsletter. “They always come back.”
Matt Walsh, a prominent right-wing provocateur who has supported Trump, said he sees nothing positive coming out of the attacks on Iran.

“What nobody has even come close to sufficiently explaining is how this war will first and foremost directly benefit American citizens,” he posted on social media. “That is a case that needed to have been made clearly and convincingly before this move, and it wasn’t. We’re also told how this will benefit Israel, and I’m sure it will. But Israel is not America. What does it do for America?”
The White House did not return a request for comment for this article.
Trump ran his 2024 campaign on the idea that foreign wars would end and his focus would be more on domestic issues.

“I will not send you to fight and die in stupid foreign wars that never end,” Trump said during a 2024 campaign stop in Pennsylvania. “I will not send our sons and daughters to go fight for a war in a country that you’ve never heard of. We’re not going to do it. We’re going to bring our troops home, and we’re going to focus on America First.”
Even Vice President JD Vance said Trump’s stance against foreign interventions was part of what drew him to the MAGA movement.

“My entire adult lifetime has been shaped by presidents who threw America into unwise wars and failed to win them,” Trump’s future vice president, JD Vance, wrote for The Wall Street Journal in a January 2023 guest column endorsing Trump’s 2024 bid, adding, “In Mr. Trump’s four years in office, he started no wars despite enormous pressure from his own party and even members of his own administration.”
The strikes have left some who worked to help elect Trump feeling both like they were duped, and worried that the war will hurt Republicans politically.

An adviser to Elon Musk’s super PAC during the 2024 campaign told NBC News that their work revolved around reaching three groups of voters who were perceived to be disillusioned with President Joe Biden and Democrats. He worried that after the war with Iran, they’ll all be lost.

“There are three tiers,” the adviser said. “One is what we called the ‘Zyn bros.’ Those who are men 18-40. The second was Hispanics, Trump did great with them in 2024. And the third was the Dearborn approach [Muslim voters]. Those are all gone.”
The person said the Iranian strikes left them feeling as though they built a message and strategy to help Trump that ended up being dishonest.
“We lied,” they said. “I am myself guilty of it. We didn’t know it’d go like this, but it turns out we lied to the voters that helped elect Trump.”
Some of the pushback around the Iran strikes has centered on Charlie Kirk, the slain co-founder of Turning Point USA, a conservative organization that focused on mobilizing younger voters.

“Last year, Charlie Kirk told us all that younger generation of Americans are far more interested in domestic policy that pursuing international conflicts and we can’t forget that in a midterm year,” posted Jack Posobiec, an influential MAGA supporter with a huge following.
A former Trump administration official told NBC News on Monday that “duration matters” in determining how the base will respond.
“Out quick and he [Trump] will be fine,” the former official said.
The former official pointed to last summer, when the U.S. made targeted strikes on Iranian nuclear facilities.

“Last summer, MAGA-types had their panties in a wad over the strikes on Iranian nuclear facilities, and it did not hurt the president,” the former official added. “Now the time horizon seems longer than one bombing, it’s hard to consider how that will impact things.”
The quickly approaching problem for Trump and Republicans are the 2026 midterms.

Republicans are trying to hold slight majorities in both the House and Senate, a task made more difficult by the fact that Trump’s approval ratings are among the worst he has seen in either term he has been in office. Trump’s approval rating stood at just 39% in an NBC News Decision Desk poll released last month.
Mike DuHaime, former Republican National Committee political director, said there is a unique dislike and distrust of Iran, which could end up working in the president’s favor.

“While there will be intense reactions from those who feel this goes against his isolationist promises, most Americans do not trust Iran and will take a wait-and-see approach to what happens next,” DuHaime said. “There will be no tolerance for American casualties, but most Americans know Iran has been an enemy of the United States and the democratic West for nearly 50 years and would therefore welcome a change.”

The administration’s rationale for bombing Iran has, at times, been inconsistent. Secretary of State Marco Rubio on Monday said there was an “imminent threat” because the U.S. believed Israel would attack Iran, which would prompt the country to retaliate against the United States. Others in the administration, though, have continued to say the move was needed to prevent Iran from getting nuclear weapons.

“President Trump will not get the United States into a years-long conflict with no clear objective,” Vice President JD Vance posted on social media Tuesday. “Iran can never be allowed to obtain a nuclear weapon. That is the goal of this operation and President Trump will see it through to completion.⁩”

Trump has expressed few concerns about alienating his political base, openly acknowledging there is no set time frame for when he expects the Iran operation to end. He also said he anticipated that service members would lose their lives.
“We expect casualties,” he told NBC News on Sunday, “but in the end it’s going to be a great deal for the world.”

title: What we know about the U.S. service members killed in the Iran war
summary: Four of six U.S. service members killed in the Iran war died when a drone struck a port in Kuwait on Sunday, U.S.
story: Four of six U.S. service members killed in the Iran war died when a drone struck a port in Kuwait on Sunday, U.S. Army Reserve Command said Tuesday.

The U.S. government has said six U.S. service members have been killed in the military operation that started early Saturday with U.S. and Israeli strikes on Iran. Iran responded by launching missiles and drones in nearby countries.
Four Army reservists died when a drone struck Port Shuabia, Kuwait, Army Reserve Command said.
They were identified Tuesday as:
Capt. Cody A. Khork, 35, of Winter Haven, Fla. Khork enlisted in the National Guard in 2009 and was commissioned as a military police officer in the Army Reserve in 2014, the military said.
Capt. Cody A. Khork, 35, of Winter Haven, Fla. Khork enlisted in the National Guard in 2009 and was commissioned as a military police officer in the Army Reserve in 2014, the military said.
Sgt. 1st Class Noah L. Tietjens, 42, of Bellevue, Neb. Tietjens joined the Army Reserve as a wheeled vehicle mechanic in 2006,
Sgt. 1st Class Noah L. Tietjens, 42, of Bellevue, Neb. Tietjens joined the Army Reserve as a wheeled vehicle mechanic in 2006,

Sgt. 1st Class Nicole M. Amor, 39, of White Bear Lake, Minn. Amor joined the National Guard in 2005 and transferred to the Army Reserve in 2006, Army Reserve Command said. She deployed to Kuwait and Iraq in 2019.

Sgt. 1st Class Nicole M. Amor, 39, of White Bear Lake, Minn. Amor joined the National Guard in 2005 and transferred to the Army Reserve in 2006, Army Reserve Command said. She deployed to Kuwait and Iraq in 2019.
Spc. Declan J. Coady, 20, West Des Moines, Iowa. Coady enlisted in the Army Reserve in 2023, the command said.
Spc. Declan J. Coady, 20, West Des Moines, Iowa. Coady enlisted in the Army Reserve in 2023, the command said.
All four were assigned to the 103rd Sustainment Command based in Des Moines, the command said.
The identities and the circumstances of the other two killed U.S. service members have not been released.
In addition to the deaths, 18 other U.S. service members have suffered serious injuries, a U.S. official told NBC News on Monday.

"We honor our fallen Heroes, who served fearlessly and selflessly in defense of our nation. Their sacrifice, and the sacrifices of their families, will never be forgotten,” Lt. Gen. Robert Harter, Chief of Army Reserve and commanding general U.S. Army Reserve Command, said in a statement.
Add NBC News to Google
Add NBC News to Google
The U.S. and Israel launched attacks in Iran around 1:15 a.m. ET Saturday, using “precision munitions launched from air, land and sea” and one-way drones, Central Command has said.
Iran’s Red Crescent said nearly 800 people have been killed in Israeli and American strikes.

Iran responded to the U.S. and Israeli strikes by launching missiles and drones at targets in surrounding nations. Eleven people have been killed in Israel, officials said, and deaths were reported in the United Arab Emirates, Kuwait and Bahrain.

Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps said Tuesday that it launched 230 attack drones on a U.S. base in Erbil, Iraq, and the U.S. Ali Al Salem and Arifjan bases in Kuwait, semi-official Iranian news agency Tasnim reported.
The military action could last weeks, President Donald Trump said Monday. “We projected four to five weeks, but we have capability to go far longer than that,” the presdient said then.
Trump and other administration officials have said that the U.S. attacked Iran because of an imminent threat and after nuclear talks failed to reach an agreement.

Some members of Congress have questioned that assessment and Trump’s power to attack Iran without seeking approval from Congress. Democrats and some Republicans plan to force a war powers vote that would restrict Trump’s ability to carry out further attacks in Iran.
Iran’s Red Crescent has said nearly 800 people were killed in Israeli and American strikes. Iran’s Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei was also killed, Iranian state media has confirmed.

title: China emerges as big winner from Supreme Court's tariff ruling
summary: HONG KONG — Global trade has been upended again after the Supreme Court struck down President Donald Trump’s “reciprocal” tariffs, with U.S. trading partners and businesses around the world grasping to understand the system that replaces them
story: HONG KONG — Global trade has been upended again after the Supreme Court struck down President Donald Trump’s “reciprocal” tariffs, with U.S. trading partners and businesses around the world grasping to understand the system that replaces them.

A new flat global tariff of 10% paid by U.S. importers took effect Tuesday, lower than the 15% that Trump said he would implement days before. Under Section 122 of the Trade Act of 1974, the 10% tariff can remain in place for 150 days without congressional approval.

China is the “biggest winner” from the Supreme Court ruling, with an effective U.S. tariff rate now much closer to that of other countries, said Alicia García-Herrero, chief economist for Asia-Pacific at French investment bank Natixis.
Among other benefits, China’s lower tariff rate reduces the incentive for companies to shift production to other countries in Asia, at least temporarily.

But the whiplash has created overwhelming uncertainty for key U.S. allies and some of Washington’s biggest trading partners, many of whom had already announced or were nearing trade deals with the U.S., some having offered major concessions with the aim of securing favorable rates under Trump’s now-defunct tariff regime.
In Asia, Trump administration officials had rushed to conclude deals in the weeks before the court ruling, with Indonesia agreeing to a 19% tariff rate only a day earlier.
The tariff ruling also comes just weeks before Trump’s upcoming trip to China, where he hopes to maintain a delicate trade truce with the world’s second-biggest economy.

Those who stand to lose the most include Japan and Taiwan, both of which had previously pledged hundreds of billions in U.S. investment in exchange for a tariff rate of 15%. Singapore and Australia also stand to lose since they already had a relatively favorable tariff rate of 10%.
“If you’re Taiwan, you’re like, why on earth did I commit to $250 billion if I’m getting the same tariffs?” said García-Herrero, who is based in Hong Kong.
The Trump administration has said that it will stand by existing deals and that it expects U.S. trading partners to do so as well.

“The good news is that almost all countries and corporations want to keep the deal that they already made,” Trump said Tuesday in his State of the Union address, “knowing that the legal power that I as president have to make a new deal could be far worse for them.”
Add NBC News to Google
Add NBC News to Google
A day earlier, Trump said in a social media post that any countries that tried to “play games” with trade deals after the court ruling would be hit with much higher tariffs.
Major trading partners in Asia, highly dependent on U.S. exports, are generally sticking within the terms of their existing deals or taking a wait-and-see approach.

India, which had planned to sign an agreement in March, delayed a scheduled trade delegation visit to Washington last week, saying talks would resume once there was more clarity. Its trade minister then had a surprise lunch meeting in New Delhi on Thursday with Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick.

While Trump officials say they expect the deals to be maintained, “point one of every deal is the reciprocal tariff rate,” said Deborah Elms, head of trade policy at the Hinrich Foundation in Singapore.
The primary question for many governments, she said, is whether their goods are now subject to their previous rates or the new 10% rate, which the White House has not clarified.
“That right there is causing a fair amount of anxiety over what is the actual rate, and then what happens to the rest of the agreement,” Elms said.
U.S. Trade Representative Jamieson Greer said last week that for some countries, which he did not name, tariff rates would rise to 15% or even higher while remaining compatible with existing deals.

He says that there are no plans to raise tariffs on Chinese goods ahead of Trump’s trip to China, which the White House says will begin March 31, and that he does not expect the Supreme Court ruling to affect Trump’s meeting with Chinese President Xi Jinping.

China, which responded to Trump’s first reciprocal tariffs last year with mounting tariffs of its own, said after the ruling that it would “comprehensively assess” any future U.S. tariff changes and adjust its countermeasures as appropriate.
“The current situation has placed China in a relatively comfortable position,” Chinese international affairs commentator Qiu Zhenhai said in a social media post.

“This is because Trump’s proposed high tariffs — whether directed at the world or specifically at China — are currently being constrained by supreme legal authorities internally and, frankly speaking, have triggered significant backlash within the United States.”
But China still has a lot to worry about, such as Trump’s ability to impose export controls on strategically vital products such as semiconductor chips.

According to Greer, one of the main ways the Trump administration plans to replace the reciprocal tariffs is through investigations, permitted under Section 301 of the 1974 Trade Act, of countries accused of treating the U.S. or its companies unfairly.

Elms said those investigations, along with national security probes focused on strategic industries under the same law, could provide the White House with a more solid basis to impose fresh tariffs, as well as other punitive measures that could end up being far worse for some countries than the tariffs previously struck down.

Though Greer has indicated a desire to move swiftly on Section 301 investigations, it would be difficult to exclude China from any list of countries facing investigations, which would risk antagonizing Beijing before Trump’s trip, Elms said.
Greer said his office was also continuing an existing Section 301 investigation into China that stems from Trump’s first-term trade deal with Beijing and could be used to justify steep new tariffs.
China said last week that it had “earnestly fulfilled its obligations” under that deal.
The Supreme Court ruling also did not affect sector-specific U.S. tariffs on automobiles and steel, which are crucial industries for China, as well as South Korea and Japan.

On Tuesday, the president of Hyundai Motor, one of South Korea’s biggest carmakers, urged lawmakers to swiftly approve the $350 billion in pledged U.S. investment, citing the risk of higher sectoral tariffs.
Elms said that many U.S. trade partners will “have domestic politics of their own” to consider.

She added: “It’s really getting very challenging to manage a government-to-government relationship with the U.S. and we will see some of that irritation, I think, spill over as the tariff rates keep changing and these deals appear to be more or less desirable.”

title: Left-wing plumber helps Greens sink ruling Labour and far right in surprise U.K. election win
summary: The Green Party delivered a stunning blow to Prime Minister Keir Starmer with a victory in a special election which also saw them beat Nigel Farage's Reform UK.
story: LONDON — Britain's beleaguered government has spent much of its time in power concerned about the surging far right. Instead, it just got a sucker punch from the progressive left.
The Green Party delivered a stunning blow to Prime Minister Keir Starmer early Friday with a victory in a hotly contested special election in the northern city of Manchester.

The vote in the constituency of Gorton and Denton was seen as a three-way tussle among the Greens, the rising far-right party Reform UK led by Trump ally Nigel Farage and Starmer's ruling center-left Labour Party, which has long dominated politics in the area.
It was a comfortable victory for Green candidate Hannah Spencer, 34, who becomes the party's fifth member of Parliament and one of 650 lawmakers in the U.K.'s lower house.

Spencer was a plumber before she entered politics, a hint of the appeal insurgents from both sides of the political spectrum now have in a country where wages are stagnant, public services are ailing and years of government scandal have eroded trust in institutions.
Starmer's position has been under intense scrutiny for months, but recent revelations linked to the Jeffrey Epstein scandal weakened him badly.

Enter the “eco-populist” Greens, who want to radically overhaul Britain’s energy system, tax multimillionaires’ wealth, dismantle its nuclear weapons program and reverse its policy on the Israeli-Palestinian conflict.

“Bear with me, because this is a lot,” the victorious Spencer told the crowd in her northern Mancunian drawl as she processed the gravity of the result. For people “who feel left behind and isolated,” she said, “I see you, and I will fight for you.”
She said she never wanted to be a politician and apologized to anyone who had booked her plumbing services.

“I am no different to every single person here in this constituency,” she said. “Working hard used to get you something. It got you a house, a nice life, holidays, it got you somewhere. But now, working hard, what does that get you?
It had been billed as a three-way race, but it wasn’t that close. Spencer got 14,980 votes, Reform’s Matthew Goodwin got 10,578, and Labour’s Angeliki Stogia got 9,364.

The result piles more pressure on Starmer, the least popular prime minister since records began, according to some polls. Even longtime supporters are dismayed at what they say is his inability to communicate a coherent message to a stagnant nation.

Saying it was a “disappointing” result and acknowledging that voters were “frustrated,” Starmer told reporters he would carry on. Asked whether he had considered resigning, he said, “I came into politics late in life to fight for change for those people who need it.” He added that he would “keep on fighting for those people for as long as I’ve got breath in my body.”
Labour “seemed almost paralyzed in terms of actually presenting a way forward on the important issues,” said Scott Lucas, a politics professor at University College Dublin.
The election also complicates the wider narrative in the United Kingdom and indeed Europe, where nationalist populists backed by the Trump administration have been surging.
The Greens have harnessed widespread anger at rising prices and the cost of living, but rather than blame immigrants, they have directed their ire against the super-rich.
“The message of giving people a better future economically can resonate; it can cut through the white noise that define so much of our politics,” Lucas said.

The local demographic is divided largely among working-class neighborhoods, college students and Muslim residents. Many of the latter two groups feel disillusioned with the direction of the government under Starmer, particularly what they see as an inadequate response to Israel's assault on Gaza.
In defeat, Goodwin typified his party’s anti-immigration and anti-Islam platform, saying in a statement, “We are losing our country,” adding that “a dangerous Muslim sectarianism has emerged.”
Spencer condemned this approach after she won.

“I won’t accept this victory tonight without calling out politicians and divisive figures who constantly scapegoat and blame our communities for all the problems in society,” she said. “My Muslim friends and neighbors are just like me: human.”
Reform still remains a national force ahead of the next national election scheduled for 2029 and has led every major opinion poll for 10 months.
However, its current average polling of 28% would not give it enough power to rule alone, and it would most likely have to enter into a coalition government.
The clear victory for the Greens cements the left-wing threat to Britain's traditional ruling parties, too.
Many pundits consider Green leader Zack Polanski a political figure who may have the potential to rival Farage’s popular appeal.
"People everywhere will now know that voting Green is the way to defeat Reform," he said in a victory statement.

title: Kim Jong Un fuels succession buzz with daughter's matching leather jacket
summary: As Kim Jong Un affirms his authority over North Korea at a rare party congress, speculation is growing that his teenage daughter Ju Ae is closer  to being named his successor.
story: Does a family that dresses together rule together?

North Korean leader Kim Jong Un, whose family has held an iron grip on the nuclear-armed state for decades, reaffirmed his authority this week at a rare meeting of the ruling Workers’ Party. But speculation is growing that he is grooming his teenage daughter to succeed him one day, fueled in part by their matching outfits at a military parade.
The parade in Pyongyang on Wednesday capped the weeklong party congress, the first since 2021, and displayed North Korea’s growing military capabilities.

State media photos released Thursday showed Kim, 42, and his daughter Kim Ju Ae standing with senior officials at a podium overlooking soldiers marching in formation. Kim and his daughter appeared in the same black leather jacket, which Kim often wears at public events.

“When his young daughter is wearing the same symbolic attire, it’s hard to see it as a coincidence,” Lim Eul-chul, a professor at the Institute for Far Eastern Studies at Kyungnam University in Seoul, told NBC News. “It is more likely to be a deliberate move to tell the North Korean public that Kim Ju Ae is her father’s heir.”

Kim Ju Ae is believed to be the second of Kim’s three children, though she is the only one who has been seen publicly. Since her first appearance in 2022, Kim — who is thought to be in her early teens — has made a number of high-profile appearances with her father, including at missile launches, New Year’s celebrations and even a military parade in Beijing.
Her growing prominence comes as North Korea has ratcheted up rhetoric against the United States and its allies.
In a speech at the parade, Kim said his military forces were “fully ready to cope with any circumstances,” according to state news agency KCNA.

title: This Oscar-nominated filmmaker wants to go back to Iran. Even if prison, and war, await.
summary: Iranian director Jafar Panahi has been confronted with a strange task in recent weeks: promoting his Oscar-nominated movie as his home country erupts in unrest and war.“On the surface, I am in a celebration,” Panahi said, speaking through a translator over Zoom from New York on Tuesday morning.
story: Iranian director Jafar Panahi has been confronted with a strange task in recent weeks: promoting his Oscar-nominated movie as his home country erupts in unrest and war.
“On the surface, I am in a celebration,” Panahi said, speaking through a translator over Zoom from New York on Tuesday morning. “But from the inside, I feel differently.”

Panahi, 65, secretly shot “It Was Just An Accident” in Iran, where authorities oversee the media and filmmakers must get their scripts approved to get film permits. The film, a revenge drama shaped by his time in the notoriously cruel Evin Prison in Tehran, is nominated in the best original screenplay and international film categories for the upcoming Oscars.

The filmmaker has been eager to share “It Was Just An Accident” with audiences, saying he considers himself “like a witness” for the events in Iran. But, he said, “when we accepted to have this campaign for the film, we never thought about these days.”

The awards season has been anything but glamorous for Panahi, whose mother and son live in Iran. He has spent months on the road promoting his film, while also struggling to absorb the fast-moving news that directly impacts his life.
“No matter how much you try to keep yourself updated from afar, it’s not the same as being there,” he said. “And you don’t know how much of it is true, how much is not true.”

In January, Panahi’s Golden Globes experience was overshadowed by a video he saw on his phone while sitting in the line of traffic going through security for the show. It was footage from Kahrizak, a morgue near Tehran that was overflowing with bodies after the Iranian regime began a crackdown on protests that left thousands dead and injured.

“Security did not allow us to leave our cars, and I had a sense of suffocation,” Panahi said. “When we arrived and [red carpet reporters] wanted to talk to me, I really did not even have the ability to speak. I kept stepping out and trying to create a balance in my mind. I kept going out to smoke.”
After the ceremony, he skipped the parties because he said he “really just could not continue.”
Less than two months later, Panahi was traveling from Barcelona to New York to tape an interview on “The Daily Show” when he learned that the U.S. and Israel had launched a joint attack on Iran.
“Maybe I had just checked my bags, I can’t remember,” he said. “I said to myself, I need to keep going for the next few days.”

With a near-total internet blackout in Iran and communications severely disrupted since Saturday’s attack, Panahi said that he was still trying to get in touch with his family there as of Tuesday morning.

In the days since the war in the Middle East began, there have been some glimmers of hope among Iranians, particularly after news broke that the strikes had killed Iran’s hard-line Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, who ruled the country with an iron grip for 36 years.

Panahi, along with 16 other artists and activists including his Oscar-nominated co-screenwriter Mehdi Mahmoudian, signed a statement in January condemning Khamenei for authorizing the “mass and systematic killing of citizens.” Iranian authorities quickly arrested Mahmoudian, who was in Tehran at the time, for signing the letter.

“Like many other people who suffered in this period, hearing about this news made me both happy and sad,” Panahi said of Khamenei’s death. “In the last statement that we issued, we said that he should step aside and he should be tried. And we are sorry that we are no longer going to have a trial for him.”

He said he is largely relying on Persian language news sources to follow events — though not everything has been on his radar. During the course of the interview, the director learned that the Israeli army had ordered residents to evacuate the neighborhood in Tehran where Evin Prison is located. The Center for Human Rights in Iran (CHRI), an independent, nonpartisan, nonprofit organization, recently issued a statement calling for Iranian prisoners to be “protected and prioritized.”

The news made Panahi immediately worry for the safety of the prisoners, including the “large number” of those who were detained after the recent protests and those who “have been incarcerated for many years.”

“What is going to happen to them?” he said. “I don’t know, and we really need to think about that. I very much hope that the situation does not push the regime to use these prisoners as human shields.”
One day, Panahi said he'll try to process what's unfolding in Iran through his craft.
“What’s happening right now, of course, is going to have its effect on me, and one day it’s going to reveal itself in one of my films.”
When Panahi does eventually return to Iran, he will likely face imprisonment again.

In December, the government sentenced him “in absentia, to one year of imprisonment, two years of prohibition from leaving the country, and a ban on membership in any political or social groups or organizations, on the charge of propaganda against the regime,” his lawyer said in a statement on X.
Flights to Iran are currently cancelled due to the conflict, but Panahi wants his press tour to end after the March 15 Oscars in L.A.

“I really do hope that there can be a way,” Panahi said of flying back to Iran. “Perhaps one reason I’m here is that my presence is going to compensate for the presence of all the people who cannot be here. And in these conversations, in these events, I could perhaps give their message to the world.”

title: Cheap, effective and battle-tested by Russia: Iran leans on Shahed drones to penetrate U.S. defenses
summary: As the United States and its Middle East allies face Tehran’s response to President Donald Trump’s renewed bombardment of Iran, they must find a solution to a growing problem: drones.
story: As the United States and its Middle East allies face Tehran’s response to President Donald Trump’s renewed bombardment of Iran, they  must find a solution to a growing problem: drones.

Cheap and simple to produce, Iran’s Shahed drones are unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) used to overwhelm air defenses in conjunction with other missiles. They have been used to successfully bombard a U.S. embassy, a radar system, an airport and a high-rise, videos on social media show. The issue, experts say, is the long-term ability to intercept them.

“The threat from one-way attack UAVs has remained persistent,” Gen. Dan Caine, the chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff, said at a briefing Monday. “Our systems have proven effective in countering these platforms, engaging targets rapidly.”

The U.S. has not released data on the munitions it faced and shot down. Information from the United Arab Emirates’ Defense Ministry shows that Iran has launched hundreds of Shahed drones at the Gulf state, of which just over 90% have been intercepted.

Those interceptions have come at a high cost.
